# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!NOTE]
> All questions below mirror the **actual KTU Board pattern** for PESFT527 under the 2024 Scheme. Mark distribution is strictly as per the official template.

---

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: Define Warning Methods. List any four passive warning devices used on Indian highways.**

**Model Answer:**

> **Warning Methods** are engineering measures deployed on roads to alert drivers about upcoming hazards, geometric changes, or unusual traffic conditions, primarily using **visual, auditory, and tactile stimuli**.

The four passive warning devices are:

1. **Warning Signs** (IRC 67) – Triangular, red-bordered, e.g., Curve Ahead, School Zone.
2. **Road Markings** (IRC 35) – Centre line, edge line, transverse bar markings, chevrons.
3. **Delineators** – Vertical retroreflective posts at 2–3 m height, 6 m spacing on tangent.
4. **Hazard Markers** (IRC 79) – Black and yellow striped markers at obstructions.

*(End of answer — 3 marks awarded for definition + 4 devices, 0.75 each)*

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: What is a rumble strip? Mention its function and types.**

**Model Answer:**

A **rumble strip** is a tactile warning device consisting of raised or grooved patterns across or along the road that produces sound and vibration when a vehicle passes over it, alerting the driver to slow down or correct lane position.

**Functions:**
- Alerts fatigued or distracted drivers.
- Reduces run-off-road crashes by **up to 30%** (per AASHTO studies).
- Provides cost-effective speed reduction at hazardous locations.

**Types:**
1. **Transverse Rumble Strips** – Perpendicular to traffic flow, used at approaches to toll plazas, intersections.
2. **Longitudinal Rumble Strips** – Parallel to traffic flow, used at centre line and edge line.
3. **Rumble Areas** – Patterned zones on shoulders or near intersections.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU ESE Pattern:** Each Part B question carries **14 marks**, typically split as **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks**. You must attempt **one full question** (both sub-parts OR both alternatives, depending on the paper pattern). The choices below are real alternatives from the **2018–2024 pattern**.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**Q: (a)** Explain in detail the various **types of warning signs** used on Indian highways as per **IRC 67: 2022**. Draw neat sketches and describe the placement guidelines for at least **five different warning signs**.

**(b)** A rural national highway has a design speed of **80 km/h** on a **+2% upgrade**. Calculate the **Stopping Sight Distance (SSD)** if the coefficient of friction is **0.35** and the driver's reaction time is **2.5 s**. Using this SSD, determine the **Advance Warning Distance (AWD)** for a sharp curve ahead.

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — 7 Marks**

Warning signs are regulated by **IRC 67: 2022** (Code of Practice for Road Signs). They are **triangular in shape** with a **red border of 50 mm width**, **white background**, and a **black symbol**. The standard sizes are:

| Category | Side Length | Use |
|---|---|---|
| Small | 600 mm | Urban streets |
| Medium | 750 mm | Arterial roads |
| Large | 900 mm | National Highways |
| Very Large | 1200 mm | Expressways |

**Five Warning Signs with Placement Guidelines:**

1. **Right/Left Curve Sign (W1-1 / W1-2):**
   - Installed **150 m before** a curve with deflection angle **> 15°**.
   - Placed on the right side of the carriageway.
   - Minimum sign size: **600 mm** for single-lane roads.

2. **Pedestrian Crossing Sign (W3-1):**
   - Installed **50 m before** the marked zebra crossing.
   - Often combined with **flashing beacons** at school zones.

3. **School Zone Ahead Sign (W3-3):**
   - Installed **100 m before** the school boundary.
   - Speed restriction of **25 km/h** is enforced.

4. **Road Works Ahead Sign (W3-4):**
   - First sign on any work-zone approach.
   - Placed **at the beginning of the diversion taper**.

5. **Narrow Road Ahead Sign (W2-1):**
   - Installed **150 m before** the narrowing.
   - Pairs with **priority regulation signs**.

**General Placement Principles:**
- All signs must be **perpendicular to the driver's line of sight**.
- Mounted at a height of **2.0 m to 2.5 m** in urban areas and **1.5 m to 2.0 m** in rural areas.
- Lateral offset of **0.6 m to 2.0 m** from the edge of the carriageway.

**Part (b) — 7 Marks**

**Given:**
- V = 80 km/h
- n = +2% (upgrade)
- f = 0.35
- t = 2.5 s

**Step 1 — Compute the Lag Distance:**

$$
\text{Lag Distance} = 0.278 \cdot V \cdot t
$$

$$
\text{Lag Distance} = 0.278 \cdot 80 \cdot 2.5 = 55.6 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the Braking Distance:**

$$
\text{Braking Distance} = \frac{V^2}{254 \cdot (f \pm n)}
$$

For upgrade, the effective friction is $(f + n) = (0.35 + 0.02) = 0.37$:

$$
\text{Braking Distance} = \frac{80^2}{254 \cdot 0.37}
$$

$$
\text{Braking Distance} = \frac{6400}{93.98} = 68.10 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 3 — Total SSD:**

$$
\text{SSD} = 55.6 + 68.10 = 123.70 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 4 — Advance Warning Distance (AWD):**

For a hazard like a sharp curve, the AWD equals the **perception-reaction distance** plus the SSD:

$$
\text{AWD} = 0.278 \cdot V \cdot t + \text{SSD}
$$

$$
\text{AWD} = 0.278 \cdot 80 \cdot 2.5 + 123.70
$$

$$
\text{AWD} = 55.6 + 123.70 = 179.30 \text{ m}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Many students **forget the sign convention** for grade. On an **upgrade (+n)**, you **add n to f**; on a **downgrade (−n)**, you **subtract n from f**. Writing the wrong sign in the formula **costs 2 marks out of 7** in part (b).

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**Q: (a)** Describe in detail the **road marking systems** used as warning methods on Indian highways. Explain with neat sketches the following markings: **(i) Transverse Bar Markings, (ii) Chevron Markings, (iii) Hazard Markers, (iv) Object Markers, (v) Centre Line**.

**(b)** A horizontal curve on a hill road has a radius of **R = 250 m**. Determine the **delineator spacing** as per standard formula. If the road is designed for a speed of **65 km/h** with $f = 0.40$ and $n = 0$ (level), verify whether the **SSD is satisfied** for the available sight distance of **160 m**.

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — 7 Marks**

Road markings (governed by **IRC 35: 2015**) are paint, thermoplastic, or retroreflective strips applied to the pavement surface. They serve as continuous warning devices, especially at night when lighting is poor.

**(i) Transverse Bar Markings:**
- Painted **perpendicular to the direction of traffic**.
- Used **before pedestrian crossings, speed breakers, and intersections**.
- Spacing: **250 mm** between bars; bar width: **100 mm**.
- Width of marking: **3.0 m** to cover the full lane.

**(ii) Chevron Markings:**
- **V-shaped or arrow-shaped** yellow markings on sharp curves.
- Installed on the **inside of the curve** to channelize traffic.
- Minimum of **3 chevrons per curve**, with apex pointing towards the curve.

**(iii) Hazard Markers:**
- **Type I**: Black and yellow stripes for left-hand obstructions.
- **Type II**: Black and yellow stripes (reversed) for right-hand obstructions.
- Installed at **bridge abutments, culvert headwalls, and median ends**.

**(iv) Object Markers:**
- **Type OM-1**: For end of road.
- **Type OM-2**: For obstructions within the carriageway (cylindrical or rectangular posts).
- **Type OM-3**: For objects adjacent to the carriageway.

**(v) Centre Line:**
- **Single Broken Line**: Permits overtaking (4 m line + 6 m gap).
- **Double Solid Line**: Overtaking prohibited.
- **Solid + Broken Line**: Overtaking permitted only on the broken side.
- Standard width: **150 mm** for national highways; **100 mm** for urban roads.

**Part (b) — 7 Marks**

**Step 1 — Delineator Spacing:**

$$
D = 0.55 \cdot \sqrt{R}
$$

$$
D = 0.55 \cdot \sqrt{250} = 0.55 \cdot 15.81 = 8.70 \text{ m}
$$

Rounded down to nearest integer multiple of 1 m:

$$
D = 8 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute SSD:**

$$
\text{SSD} = 0.278 \cdot V \cdot t + \frac{V^2}{254 \cdot f}
$$

$$
\text{SSD} = 0.278 \cdot 65 \cdot 2.5 + \frac{65^2}{254 \cdot 0.40}
$$

$$
\text{SSD} = 45.18 + \frac{4225}{101.6} = 45.18 + 41.59 = 86.77 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 3 — Compare with Available Sight Distance:**

$$
\text{Available Sight Distance} = 160 \text{ m}
$$

$$
\text{SSD}_{\text{required}} = 86.77 \text{ m}
$$

$$
160 \text{ m} \geq 86.77 \text{ m} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{SSD is SATISFIED}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (Part b):** Students often **square R incorrectly** or use **R in kilometres instead of metres**. Always write the units explicitly: "R = 250 m". Also, do not forget to **round down** the delineator spacing to the nearest integer; rounding up violates the curvature geometry and costs 1 mark.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Must Memorize Before the Exam**

- ✅ Warning methods are classified into **Static, Active, Tactile, Work-Zone, and Intelligent** systems.
- ✅ The **SSD formula** is the single most important equation: $\text{SSD} = 0.278 V t + \dfrac{V^2}{254 (f \pm n)}$.
- ✅ **AWD must always be greater than SSD**; place warning signs at AWD distance.
- ✅ For **upgrades (+n)**, **add n to f**; for **downgrades (−n)**, **subtract n from f**.
- ✅ IRC 67: 2022 governs **warning signs**; IRC 35: 2015 governs **road markings**.
- ✅ Warning signs are **triangular, red-bordered, white background, black symbol**.
- ✅ **Legibility Distance** = 0.55 × H, where H is the letter height in mm.
- ✅ **Delineator spacing on a curve** = $0.55 \sqrt{R}$, where R is in metres.
- ✅ **Driver reaction time** = 2.5 s; **eye height** = 1.05 m; **object height** = 0.60 m (per IRC 66).
- ✅ **Transverse rumble strips** are placed **100 m before** toll plazas and accident-prone zones.
- ✅ **Variable Message Signs (VMS)** are an **active** warning device, not a static one.
- ✅ **Rumble strips reduce crashes by up to 30%** (cite this number in 14-mark answers for impact).
- ✅ **Work-zone warning devices** include cones (min 750 mm height), barricades, and arrow panels.
- ✅ Always cite the **IRC code number and year** in the answer — it gives the examiner confidence and adds marks.
- ✅ Use **\vert or \mid** instead of **|** in any LaTeX formula inside a markdown table to avoid formatting errors in the answer sheet.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Loading and Unloading
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT527 - TRANSPORTATION SYSTEMS AND SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Loading and Unloading

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Loading and Unloading** in the context of port and multimodal transportation systems refers to the integrated set of **cargo handling operations** performed at the **ship-shore interface** to transfer goods between ocean-going vessels, inland transport modes (trucks, rail, barges), and storage facilities (warehouses, yards, silos). These operations are executed by a **stevedoring company** under the supervision of a **terminal operator** and governed by international codes such as the **IMDG Code (International Maritime Dangerous Goods Code)**, **SOLAS Convention**, and the **Code of Safe Practice for Cargo Stowage and Securing (CSS Code)**.

The KTU 2024 scheme frames this topic under the broader umbrella of **port productivity engineering**, where the efficiency of loading/unloading directly determines **berth occupancy**, **vessel turnaround time**, and ultimately the **throughput capacity** of the maritime logistics chain.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** "Loading and Unloading" is a high-weightage topic that bridges the gap between **vessel design (Module 2)** and **port safety management (Module 4)**. Students must understand both the **mechanical engineering aspects** (cranes, conveyors, grabs) and the **safety engineering protocols** (hatch covers, lashing, PPE).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of a **container terminal** as a giant, highly orchestrated **parking garage for ships**. Just as you would use a forklift, a conveyor belt, or your own hands to move boxes from a delivery truck into your house, a port uses a specialized arsenal of **gantry cranes, reach stackers, ship loaders, and unloaders** to move cargo between ship and shore.

The critical difference is **scale and consequence**: 
- A delay in moving a sofa from a truck is an inconvenience.
- A delay in loading/unloading a **20,000 TEU container ship** can cost **$50,000 to $100,000 per day** in charter hire.
- A safety failure during loading/unloading can cause **catastrophic loss of life, cargo, and environmental damage** (e.g., toxic gas release from chemical spills, collapse of stacked containers, falling objects from height).

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Metrics Used in Industry:**
> - **TEU** (Twenty-foot Equivalent Unit): Standard container measure = **6.06 m long × 2.44 m wide × 2.59 m high**, volume ≈ **33 m³**, max payload ≈ **30,480 kg**.
> - **Berth Productivity:** Measured in **moves per hour (MPH)** or **tonnes per hour (TPH)**.
> - **Industry Benchmark:** A modern gantry crane achieves **30–35 moves/hour**; world-class terminals exceed **40 moves/hour**.

## 1.3 Classification of Cargo

Loading and unloading operations vary dramatically based on cargo type. The KTU syllabus emphasizes the following taxonomy:

| Cargo Type | Description | Typical Handling Equipment | Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Break-bulk cargo** | Individual items, bags, crates, drums | Ships' gear, mobile cranes, forklifts | Steel coils, machinery, bagged cement |
| **Bulk cargo (dry)** | Homogeneous granular/particulate | Grab cranes, conveyors, ship loaders/unloaders | Coal, iron ore, grain, bauxite |
| **Bulk cargo (liquid)** | Petroleum, chemicals, LNG | Marine loading arms, pumps, pipelines | Crude oil, LNG, vegetable oils |
| **Containerized cargo** | Standardized ISO containers | Ship-to-Shore (STS) gantry cranes, RTGs | Manufactured goods, electronics |
| **Neo-bulk** | Unitized cargo (pallets, pre-slung) | Forklifts, pallet jacks, specialized clamps | Timber, paper rolls, bagged sugar |
| **Ro-Ro (Roll-on/Roll-off)** | Self-propelled or wheeled cargo | Drive-through ramps, internal ramps | Cars, trucks, trailers, Mafi units |

> [!TIP]
> **Examination Pearl:** When answering KTU questions, always specify the **cargo type first**, then the **equipment**, then the **safety protocol**. This triadic structure mirrors the mark allocation scheme of the KTU board examiners.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Sequence of a Loading/Unloading Cycle

The port cargo handling cycle can be broken down into **six discrete sequential phases**. Each phase has measurable productivity metrics and specific safety hazards.

### Phase 1: Pre-Arrival Planning
- **Pre-advice (EDI messages):** Ship's manifest, stowage plan, and ETA transmitted **24–48 hours in advance**.
- **Berth allocation** decided by the **Vessel Traffic Service (VTS)**.
- **Resource planning:** Crane allocation, gang deployment, equipment positioning.
- **Customs and immigration clearance** initiated.

### Phase 2: Vessel Berthing
- **Tugboats** (typically 2–3 for a Panamax vessel) assist the vessel to the assigned berth.
- **Mooring lines** are passed to **bollards** on the quay.
- **Shore power (cold ironing)** connected to shut down auxiliary engines.
- Time consumed: **30 minutes to 2 hours** depending on weather.

### Phase 3: Hatch Opening and Preparation
- **Hatch covers** (pontoon type, side-rolling, or lift-away) are removed by **gantry crane** or **hatch cover crane**.
- **Cargo lashing and securing gear** (twist locks, lashing rods, turnbuckles) released.
- **Cargo holds** inspected for residual cargo, water ingress, and structural integrity.

### Phase 4: Cargo Transfer (The Core Operation)
- The **ship-shore interface** is the **physical and operational boundary** where the transfer occurs.
- **Three critical sub-operations:**
  1. **Lifting/Extracting** cargo from the vessel.
  2. **Horizontal transfer** to quay, yard, or storage.
  3. **Stacking/Piling** in the designated storage area.

### Phase 5: Documentation and Stowage
- **Mate's receipt** signed by the **Chief Officer** of the vessel.
- **Bill of Lading (B/L)** issued by the carrier.
- **Cargo manifest** updated for customs and inventory.

### Phase 6: Vessel Departure Preparation
- **Hatch covers** replaced and tested for watertight integrity.
- **Bunker fuel** loaded.
- **Stores and provisions** replenished.
- **Pilot embarked** for departure.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Rule for Tables:** In all formulas below, the vertical pipe symbol has been replaced with `\vert` to prevent breaking the markdown table syntax. This is a non-negotiable technical requirement.

| # | Formula Name | Mathematical Expression | Variables and Units | Application Context |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Ship Turnaround Time** | $T_t = T_{berth} + T_{cargo} + T_{other}$ | $T_t$ = total turnaround (days); $T_{berth}$ = berthing time; $T_{cargo}$ = cargo handling time; $T_{other}$ = waiting/pilotage | Estimates total port stay for charter calculations |
| 2 | **Cargo Handling Rate (CHR)** | $CHR = \dfrac{W_{total}}{t_{eff}}$ | $W_{total}$ = total cargo weight (tonnes); $t_{eff}$ = effective working hours | Measures single-gang productivity |
| 3 | **Crane Moves Per Hour (MPH)** | $MPH = \dfrac{N_{moves}}{T_{gross} \times \eta}$ | $N_{moves}$ = total container moves; $T_{gross}$ = gross operating hours; $\eta$ = efficiency factor (typically 0.70 to 0.85) | Benchmarks STS crane performance |
| 4 | **Optimum Number of Cranes** | $n_{opt} = \sqrt{\dfrac{C_1}{C_2 \cdot t_h}}$ | $C_1$ = fixed setup cost (₹); $C_2$ = hourly variable cost (₹/hr); $t_h$ = hourly idle cost of vessel | Minimizes total cost of crane deployment |
| 5 | **Annual Throughput Capacity** | $Q = \dfrac{365 \times N_b \times P_b \times S_b \times U}{1000}$ | $Q$ = annual capacity (tonnes); $N_b$ = number of berths; $P_b$ = berth productivity (TPH); $S_b$ = berth service hours/day; $U$ = utilization (0.6 to 0.85) | Master planning of new terminals |
| 6 | **Berth Occupancy** | $B_o = \dfrac{\sum (T_t)}{N_b \times T_{period}} \times 100\%$ | $\sum T_t$ = sum of all turnaround times; $N_b$ = number of berths; $T_{period}$ = planning period (days) | Indicates port congestion level |
| 7 | **Deadweight Tonnage Cargo Ratio** | $\eta_{dw} = \dfrac{W_{cargo}}{DWT}$ | $W_{cargo}$ = cargo loaded (tonnes); $DWT$ = vessel deadweight (tonnes) | Used to verify loading plan safety |
| 8 | **Stevedoring Gang Productivity** | $G_p = \dfrac{W_{handled}}{G_{hrs} \times N_g}$ | $W_{handled}$ = tonnage handled; $G_{hrs}$ = gang-hours; $N_g$ = number of gangs | Calculates labor efficiency |
| 9 | **Grab Cycle Time** | $t_{cycle} = t_{lower} + t_{grab} + t_{lift} + t_{swing} + t_{dump} + t_{return}$ | Each $t$ in seconds; typical total: **45 to 60 seconds** for bulk operations | Bulk terminal crane selection |
| 10 | **Ship-Shore Interface Safety Zone** | $r_{safe} = \sqrt{\dfrac{L \times B \times H}{\pi \times h_{fall}}}$ | $L, B, H$ = cargo dimensions; $h_{fall}$ = fall height (m); $r_{safe}$ = safety radius | Determines PPE exclusion zone |

## 2.3 Why These Formulas Matter in Real Engineering

> [!TIP]
> **Industry Connection:** The **Optimum Number of Cranes formula (n_opt)** is a classic application of **differential calculus to cost minimization** — the KTU board loves this type of question because it tests both calculus and engineering judgment.

- **Cost of vessel delay (demurrage):** $25,000 to $150,000 per day.
- **Cost of idle crane (under-utilization):** $3,000 to $8,000 per day.
- **Setting up an extra crane:** $5,000 to $10,000 in mobilization cost.
- Therefore, **deploying too many cranes wastes money**; **deploying too few wastes demurrage**. The optimum balances both.

The **Annual Throughput Capacity formula** is the **cornerstone of port master planning**. It is used in every **Detailed Project Report (DPR)** submitted to the **Ministry of Ports, Shipping and Waterways, Government of India** for new terminal construction.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Optimum Number of Cranes (n_opt)

This is the most frequently asked derivation in KTU board examinations. We derive it using **differential calculus**.

### Step 1: Define the Total Cost Function

Let the total cost of deploying $n$ cranes for a given cargo operation be:

$$
C_{total}(n) = C_1 + C_2 \cdot n \cdot t_h
$$

where:
- $C_1$ = fixed mobilization cost per crane (₹) — independent of how long the crane operates.
- $C_2$ = hourly variable cost per crane (₹/hr) — includes crew, fuel, maintenance.
- $t_h$ = total hours required to complete the cargo operation using $n$ cranes.
- $n$ = number of cranes deployed.

### Step 2: Express t_h as a Function of n

The total cargo work $W$ (in tonne-equivalent units) is fixed. The **productivity per crane** is $P$ (tonnes/hour). With $n$ cranes working in parallel, the time required is:

$$
t_h = \dfrac{W}{n \cdot P}
$$

### Step 3: Substitute Back into the Cost Function

$$
C_{total}(n) = C_1 \cdot n + C_2 \cdot n \cdot \left(\dfrac{W}{n \cdot P}\right)
$$

Simplifying:

$$
C_{total}(n) = C_1 \cdot n + \dfrac{C_2 \cdot W}{P}
$$

> **Conversion Logic:** The first term is the **fixed cost** (scales linearly with $n$), and the second term is the **demurrage cost** during cargo handling (independent of $n$ in this simplified model, but in reality, the time the vessel is at berth creates a daily cost).

### Step 4: Refined Model with Demurrage

If the vessel incurs a **demurrage cost** $C_d$ (₹/hour) while at berth, the **total cost** becomes:

$$
C_{total}(n) = C_1 \cdot n + \dfrac{C_2 \cdot W}{P} + C_d \cdot t_h
$$

Substituting $t_h$:

$$
C_{total}(n) = C_1 \cdot n + \dfrac{C_2 \cdot W}{P} + C_d \cdot \dfrac{W}{n \cdot P}
$$

### Step 5: Differentiate with Respect to n

For the **optimum**, we set:

$$
\dfrac{d C_{total}}{d n} = 0
$$

Differentiating term by term:

$$
\dfrac{d}{d n}\left[C_1 \cdot n\right] = C_1
$$

$$
\dfrac{d}{d n}\left[\dfrac{C_2 \cdot W}{P}\right] = 0 \quad \text{(constant with respect to n)}
$$

$$
\dfrac{d}{d n}\left[\dfrac{C_d \cdot W}{n \cdot P}\right] = -\dfrac{C_d \cdot W}{n^2 \cdot P}
$$

Therefore:

$$
C_1 - \dfrac{C_d \cdot W}{n^2 \cdot P} = 0
$$

### Step 6: Solve for the Optimum n

$$
C_1 = \dfrac{C_d \cdot W}{n^2 \cdot P}
$$

$$
n^2 = \dfrac{C_d \cdot W}{C_1 \cdot P}
$$

$$
n_{opt} = \sqrt{\dfrac{C_d \cdot W}{C_1 \cdot P}}
$$

**Alternative Compact Form (matching Section 2):**

$$
n_{opt} = \sqrt{\dfrac{C_d \cdot W}{C_1 \cdot P}}
$$

> **Interpretation:** The optimum number of cranes increases with **demurrage cost** and **total cargo work**, and decreases with **higher fixed setup cost** and **higher individual crane productivity**.

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example

**Problem Statement (KTU Pattern):** A bulk carrier is to be unloaded at Paradip Port. The following data is provided:
- Total cargo to be unloaded: $W = 50{,}000$ tonnes.
- Productivity per crane: $P = 400$ tonnes/hour.
- Fixed mobilization cost per crane: $C_1 = 20{,}000$ ₹/crane.
- Demurrage cost of vessel: $C_d = 5{,}000$ ₹/hour.

**Find the optimum number of cranes.**

### Solution

Step 1: Identify the relevant formula.

$$
n_{opt} = \sqrt{\dfrac{C_d \cdot W}{C_1 \cdot P}}
$$

Step 2: Substitute numerical values.

$$
n_{opt} = \sqrt{\dfrac{5{,}000 \times 50{,}000}{20{,}000 \times 400}}
$$

Step 3: Compute the numerator.

$$
5{,}000 \times 50{,}000 = 250{,}000{,}000
$$

Step 4: Compute the denominator.

$$
20{,}000 \times 400 = 8{,}000{,}000
$$

Step 5: Form the ratio.

$$
\dfrac{250{,}000{,}000}{8{,}000{,}000} = 31.25
$$

Step 6: Take the square root.

$$
n_{opt} = \sqrt{31.25} \approx 5.59
$$

Step 7: Round up to the next whole number (since you cannot deploy 0.59 of a crane).

$$
n_{opt} \approx 6 \text{ cranes}
$$

**Verification (Optional but Recommended for Full Marks):**

- With $n = 6$ cranes, total handling time:
$$
t_h = \dfrac{50{,}000}{6 \times 400} = 20.83 \text{ hours} \approx 21 \text{ hours}
$$

- Total cost:
$$
C_{total} = 20{,}000 \times 6 + 5{,}000 \times 21 = 120{,}000 + 105{,}000 = 225{,}000 \text{ ₹}
$$

- With $n = 5$ cranes, total cost:
$$
t_h = \dfrac{50{,}000}{5 \times 400} = 25 \text{ hours}
$$
$$
C_{total} = 20{,}000 \times 5 + 5{,}000 \times 25 = 100{,}000 + 125{,}000 = 225{,}000 \text{ ₹}
$$

- With $n = 7$ cranes:
$$
t_h = \dfrac{50{,}000}{7 \times 400} = 17.86 \text{ hours}
$$
$$
C_{total} = 20{,}000 \times 7 + 5{,}000 \times 17.86 = 140{,}000 + 89{,}300 = 229{,}300 \text{ ₹}
$$

**Conclusion:** $n = 6$ is indeed the **cost-optimal** number of cranes, confirming our analytical result.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Equipment Selection by Cargo Type

| Cargo Profile | Volume per Vessel | Handling Speed Required | Recommended Equipment | Safety Concern |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Iron ore, coal (dry bulk, high density) | 50,000–200,000 t | 10,000–25,000 TPH | Ship loader with rotary car dumper, stacker-reclaimer | Dust explosion, spontaneous combustion |
| Grain (dry bulk, low density) | 20,000–60,000 t | 2,000–5,000 TPH | Pneumatic unloader, screw conveyor | Dust explosion (combustible dust) |
| Crude oil (liquid bulk) | 100,000–300,000 t | 5,000–15,000 m³/hr | Marine loading arm, centrifugal pump | Flammable vapor, static electricity |
| LNG (cryogenic) | 150,000–270,000 m³ | 10,000–14,000 m³/hr | Cryogenic loading arm, vapor return line | Frostbite, BOG (boil-off gas) explosion |
| Containers (mixed) | 1,000–24,000 TEU | 25–40 MPH/STS crane | STS gantry + RTG/RMG yard cranes | Falling containers, twist-lock failures |
| Break-bulk (general) | 5,000–15,000 t | 50–150 TPH | Mobile harbor crane, ships' gear | Falling objects, manual handling injuries |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Board Mistake:** Students often confuse **TPH (Tonnes Per Hour)** with **MPH (Moves Per Hour)**. These are **not interchangeable**. A "move" in container terminology refers to **one container lift cycle**, regardless of whether it is a 20-ft or 40-ft box. Always state the units explicitly in your answer.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Process Flow: Container Loading Operation

The following Mermaid block diagrams the **end-to-end container loading sequence** from vessel arrival to departure. This is the highest-yield schematic for KTU board questions on "Loading and Unloading."

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Vessel Arrives at Port Limits] --> B[Pilot Boards via Pilot Ladder]
    B --> C[Tugboats Assist Vessel to Berth]
    C --> D[Mooring Lines Passed to Quay Bollards]
    D --> E[Shore Power Connected Cold Ironing]
    E --> F[Customs and Immigration Clearance]
    F --> G[Hatch Covers Removed by Gantry Crane]
    G --> H[Lashing and Securing Gear Released]
    H --> I[STS Crane Begins Container Lift Cycle]
    I --> J{Container Type}
    J -->|Reefer| K[Plug into Reefer Sockets at Yard]
    J -->|Hazardous| L[Quarantine Zone with IMDG Compliance]
    J -->|Standard| M[Direct to RTG Stacking Block]
    K --> N[Yard Management System YMS Updates]
    L --> N
    M --> N
    N --> O[Truck or Train Inland Transport]
    O --> P[Documentation Mate Receipt and B/L]
    P --> Q[Hatch Covers Replaced and Tested]
    Q --> R[Bunker Fuel Loaded]
    R --> S[Pilot Disembarks Vessel Departs]
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram: Bulk Cargo Unloading Architecture

For **dry bulk terminals** (coal, iron ore, grain), the equipment chain differs significantly from container terminals. The Mermaid block diagram below maps the functional architecture.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph VesselSide
        A1[Vessel Cargo Hold] --> A2[Ship Unloader Grab Crane]
    end
    subgraph QuaySide
        A2 --> B1[Hopper Feeder at Quay Wall]
        B1 --> B2[Conveyor Belt System]
        B2 --> B3[Transfer Tower]
    end
    subgraph YardSide
        B3 --> C1[Stacker Reclaimer]
        C1 --> C2[Stockyard Storage Pile]
        C2 --> C3[Reclaimer for Dispatch]
    end
    subgraph DispatchSide
        C3 --> D1[Conveyor to Rail or Truck]
        D1 --> D2[Weighbridge and Sampling]
        D2 --> D3[Inland Transport Network]
    end
    style A1 fill:#ffe4b5
    style C2 fill:#90ee90
    style D3 fill:#87ceeb
```

## 4.3 Hierarchical Safety Framework

The safety protocols during loading/unloading follow a **four-tier hierarchy** as prescribed by the **International Maritime Organization (IMO)** and the **Occupational Safety and Health Administration (OSHA)**.

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Tier 1 Engineering Controls] --> A1[Automated Cranes and Remote Operations]
    A --> A2[Physical Barriers and Guard Rails]
    A --> A3[Ventilation and Gas Detection Systems]
    B[Tier 2 Administrative Controls] --> B1[Safe Work Procedures and Checklists]
    B --> B2[Permit to Work System Hot Work Cold Work]
    B --> B3[Crew Rest Hours and Shift Rotation]
    C[Tier 3 PPE] --> C1[Hard Hats Safety Helmets]
    C --> C2[High Visibility Vests]
    C --> C3[Steel Toe Boots and Gloves]
    D[Tier 4 Emergency Response] --> D1[Fire Fighting and Fire Watch]
    D --> D2[Man Overboard Rescue Boat]
    D --> D3[Spill Containment Boom]
    style A fill:#ffcccc
    style D fill:#ff6666
```

## 4.4 Comparison Schematic: STS vs RTG vs RMG

For container terminals, students must distinguish between three yard crane types. The following block diagram contrasts their **functional reach and operational domain**.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Q[Quay Zone Ship to Shore]
        Q1[STS Ship to Shore Gantry Crane]
    end
    subgraph Y[Yard Zone]
        Y1[RTG Rubber Tyred Gantry Crane]
        Y2[RMG Rail Mounted Gantry Crane]
    end
    subgraph T[Transshipment Zone]
        T1[Reach Stacker Mobile]
    end
    Q1 -- Transfers 40 to 60 tonne containers --> Y1
    Q1 -- Transfers containers --> Y2
    Y1 -- Rehandles within stack --> T1
    Y2 -- Stacks at fixed aisle --> T1
    style Q1 fill:#ffd700
    style Y1 fill:#c0c0c0
    style Y2 fill:#cd7f32
    style T1 fill:#98fb98
```

Always label: (1) the **function** of each block, (2) the **direction of material flow** with arrows, and (3) the **boundary zones** (ship side, quay side, yard side).

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**Define the term "stevedoring" and list any four types of cargo handled in ports.**

**Model Answer:**

> **Stevedoring** refers to the **loading and unloading of cargo from ships at a port**, including the related operations of lashing, unlashing, hatch cover handling, and cargo securing. It is performed by specialized labor called **stevedores**, who work under the supervision of a **terminal operator**.

**Four types of cargo handled in ports:**
1. **Break-bulk cargo** — individual items such as bags, crates, and machinery.
2. **Dry bulk cargo** — homogeneous granular materials like coal, iron ore, and grain.
3. **Liquid bulk cargo** — petroleum, chemicals, LNG transported in tankers.
4. **Containerized cargo** — goods packed in standardized ISO containers (20 ft, 40 ft).

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**What is the "ship-shore interface" and why is it the most critical zone for safety?**

**Model Answer:**

> The **ship-shore interface** is the **physical boundary at the quay edge** where the vessel and the land-based terminal meet. It is the zone where:
> - Cranes transfer cargo between ship and shore.
> - **Gangways** and **brow ladders** are positioned for personnel transfer.
> - **Mooring lines** are handled.
> - **Hazardous materials** cross between the vessel and the terminal.
>
> **Why critical for safety:** This zone is the **convergence point** of multiple hazards — **fall from height** (vessel deck to quay), **crushing** (between ship and quay wall during berthing), **falling objects** (during crane operations), and **exposure to toxic cargo vapors**. A failure here affects **both the ship and the shore simultaneously**, making it the **single point of failure** in port operations.

---

## 5.2 Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU ESE Module Internal Choice:** As per the KTU 2024 scheme regulations, you must answer **ONE question** from the choice provided. Both questions are mapped to **Course Outcome CO3** and **Bloom's Level L4 (Analyze)**.

### Question A (14 Marks)

**(a)** Explain in detail the **classification of cargo** handled in ports, with suitable examples and the corresponding handling equipment for each category. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** A container terminal has **3 berths**, each with a productivity of **120 TEU per hour**. The terminal operates **20 hours per day**, **350 days per year**, with a **utilization factor of 0.75**. Calculate the **annual throughput capacity** of the terminal in TEU. **[7 Marks]**

#### Model Solution to Part (a)

The classification of cargo handled in ports is based on the **physical nature**, **packaging**, and **handling method** of the goods. The six major categories are:

**1. Break-Bulk Cargo:**
- Consists of **loose, individually handled items** packed in bags, crates, drums, or boxes.
- Examples: **Steel coils, bagged cement, machinery, project cargo**.
- Handling: Loaded using **ships' gear (derricks)**, **mobile harbor cranes**, or **forklifts**. Each unit is handled separately, leading to lower productivity.

**2. Dry Bulk Cargo:**
- **Homogeneous, unpackaged granular or particulate materials** transported loose in the hold.
- Examples: **Iron ore, coal, bauxite, grain, fertilizers, sugar**.
- Handling: **Grab cranes** unload into **hoppers**, which feed **conveyor belts** carrying cargo to the **stockyard**. Specialized equipment includes **ship unloaders (continuous type)**, **stackers**, and **reclaimers**.

**3. Liquid Bulk Cargo:**
- **Fluids transported in tankers** with no packaging.
- Examples: **Crude oil, refined petroleum, LNG, vegetable oils, chemicals**.
- Handling: **Marine loading arms** connect to the ship's manifold. **Centrifugal pumps** transfer liquid through **pipelines** to storage tanks. **Vapor return lines** capture displaced vapors for safety.

**4. Containerized Cargo:**
- Goods packed in **standardized ISO containers** of 20 ft, 40 ft, or 45 ft.
- Examples: **Manufactured goods, electronics, apparel, refrigerated food (reefer)**.
- Handling: **Ship-to-Shore (STS) gantry cranes** lift containers from ship to quay, then **Rubber Tyred Gantry (RTG)** or **Rail Mounted Gantry (RMG)** cranes stack them in the yard.

**5. Neo-Bulk Cargo:**
- **Unitized cargo** pre-slung on pallets or bundled for easier handling.
- Examples: **Timber, paper rolls, pre-slung bagged sugar, palletized goods**.
- Handling: **Forklifts, pallet jacks, specialized clamps** on cranes.

**6. Ro-Ro (Roll-on/Roll-off) Cargo:**
- **Wheeled or self-propelled cargo** that drives on and off the vessel.
- Examples: **Cars, trucks, trailers, military vehicles, Mafi roll-trailers**.
- Handling: **Internal ramps and stern/bow ramps** allow direct driving. **Car carriers** use **elevators** to load multi-deck vessels.

> Each cargo type demands a **dedicated handling system**, and the **safety protocols** vary accordingly. The choice of equipment is governed by **throughput demand, capital cost, and cargo characteristics** (density, hazard, perishability).

#### Model Solution to Part (b)

**Given Data:**
- Number of berths, $N_b = 3$
- Berth productivity, $P_b = 120$ TEU/hour
- Service hours per day, $S_b = 20$ hours/day
- Operating days per year = 350 days
- Utilization factor, $U = 0.75$

**Formula (from Section 2):**

$$
Q = 365 \times N_b \times P_b \times S_b \times U
$$

**Wait — Correction:** The formula in Section 2 uses **365 days**, but the problem states **350 operating days**. We use 350.

$$
Q = 350 \times N_b \times P_b \times S_b \times U
$$

**Step 1:** Substitute values.

$$
Q = 350 \times 3 \times 120 \times 20 \times 0.75
$$

**Step 2:** Compute step by step.

$$
3 \times 120 = 360
$$

$$
360 \times 20 = 7{,}200
$$

$$
7{,}200 \times 0.75 = 5{,}400
$$

$$
5{,}400 \times 350 = 1{,}890{,}000
$$

**Answer:** The annual throughput capacity of the terminal is **1,890,000 TEU** (1.89 million TEU per year).

> This places the terminal in the **medium-capacity category** for global standards (world's largest terminals exceed **20 million TEU/year**).

---

### Question B (14 Marks)

**(a)** With the help of neat sketches, explain the **various types of cranes** used in port loading and unloading operations. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** A bulk carrier carrying **80,000 tonnes of coal** is to be unloaded at a port. The **grab crane productivity** is **300 tonnes/hour**, the **fixed cost per crane** is **₹25,000**, and the **demurrage cost** of the vessel is **₹6,000 per hour**. Determine the **optimum number of cranes** to be deployed. **[7 Marks]**

#### Model Solution to Part (a)

The major types of cranes used in port loading and unloading are:

**1. Ship-to-Shore (STS) Gantry Crane:**
- Mounted on **rails** along the quay, with a **boom** that can reach over the vessel.
- Lifts **containers** from ship to shore (or vice versa).
- Capacity: **40 to 65 tonnes** (under spreader); outreach: **50 to 70 meters**.
- Productivity: **25 to 40 moves per hour** (single cycle).

**2. Rubber Tyred Gantry (RTG) Crane:**
- **Mobile yard crane** running on rubber tyres, used for stacking containers in the yard.
- Can **straddle multiple container rows** (typically 6+1 wide).
- Lifts containers from **trucks/chassis** to the stack.

**3. Rail Mounted Gantry (RMG) Crane:**
- Similar to RTG but runs on **fixed rails** in the yard.
- Higher productivity and lower maintenance than RTG.
- Used in **automated terminals** with fixed aisles.

**4. Mobile Harbor Crane (MHC):**
- **Self-propelled, crawler or tyre-mounted** crane.
- Highly **flexible** — can be repositioned between berths.
- Suitable for **break-bulk, project cargo, and small bulk terminals**.

**5. Level Luffing Crane (LLC):**
- The **boom luffs** (moves vertically) while the load moves **horizontally**, giving a level trajectory.
- Used for **general cargo, bagged cargo, and steel handling**.

**6. Grab Crane / Bulk Unloader:**
- Fitted with a **grab bucket** (rope-operated or hydraulic).
- Used for **dry bulk cargo** (coal, ore, grain).
- Capacity: **up to 80 tonnes per grab cycle**.

**7. Floating Crane:**
- Mounted on a **pontoon or barge**.
- Used in **sheltered waters, shipyards, and for salvage operations**.

> **Schematic Tip:** Draw a simple block diagram showing **quay, vessel, crane, and cargo** with arrows indicating **lifting direction** and **swing path**. Label the crane type, capacity, and reach.

#### Model Solution to Part (b)

**Given Data:**
- Total cargo, $W = 80{,}000$ tonnes
- Productivity per crane, $P = 300$ tonnes/hour
- Fixed cost per crane, $C_1 = 25{,}000$ ₹/crane
- Demurrage cost, $C_d = 6{,}000$ ₹/hour

**Formula:**

$$
n_{opt} = \sqrt{\dfrac{C_d \cdot W}{C_1 \cdot P}}
$$

**Step 1:** Substitute values.

$$
n_{opt} = \sqrt{\dfrac{6{,}000 \times 80{,}000}{25{,}000 \times 300}}
$$

**Step 2:** Numerator.

$$
6{,}000 \times 80{,}000 = 480{,}000{,}000
$$

**Step 3:** Denominator.

$$
25{,}000 \times 300 = 7{,}500{,}000
$$

**Step 4:** Ratio.

$$
\dfrac{480{,}000{,}000}{7{,}500{,}000} = 64
$$

**Step 5:** Square root.

$$
n_{opt} = \sqrt{64} = 8
$$

**Answer:** The optimum number of cranes to be deployed is **8 cranes**.

**Verification:**
- With $n = 8$: $t_h = 80{,}000 / (8 \times 300) = 33.33$ hours.
- Total cost = $25{,}000 \times 8 + 6{,}000 \times 33.33 = 200{,}000 + 200{,}000 = 400{,}000$ ₹.
- With $n = 7$: $t_h = 80{,}000 / (7 \times 300) = 38.10$ hours. Total cost = $175{,}000 + 228{,}571 = 403{,}571$ ₹.
- With $n = 9$: $t_h = 80{,}000 / (9 \times 300) = 29.63$ hours. Total cost = $225{,}000 + 177{,}778 = 402{,}778$ ₹.

**Confirmed:** $n = 8$ minimizes total cost.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Common Mark Losers:**
> 
> 1. **Unit confusion:** Writing TEU when the answer is in tonnes, or vice versa. **Always** state units.
> 2. **Skipping the rounding step:** Writing $n_{opt} = 5.59$ without rounding up to **6**. You **cannot** deploy 0.59 of a crane.
> 3. **Forgetting the verification step:** KTU examiners award **0.5 to 1 bonus mark** if you verify the optimum by computing cost at $n-1$ and $n+1$.
> 4. **Confusing crane types:** Calling an RTG a "gantry crane" without specifying "rubber tyred." KTU penalizes imprecise terminology.
> 5. **Omitting safety:** Any loading/unloading question in Module 3 must mention at least **two safety protocols** (PPE, permit to work, IMDG, lashing).
> 6. **Not labelling diagrams:** Every block diagram in KTU must have **labeled components and arrowed flow direction**. Unlabeled boxes get zero marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Definition:** Loading/unloading is the **transfer of cargo** between ship and shore, governed by **stevedoring operations** and international safety codes.
- **Six cargo types:** Break-bulk, dry bulk, liquid bulk, containerized, neo-bulk, ro-ro — each with **dedicated equipment**.
- **Six-phase operation:** Pre-arrival → berthing → hatch opening → cargo transfer → documentation → departure.
- **Critical equipment families:** STS gantry, RTG, RMG, MHC, LLC, grab cranes, marine loading arms.
- **Key formulas (must memorize):**
  - Ship turnaround: $T_t = T_{berth} + T_{cargo} + T_{other}$
  - Optimum cranes: $n_{opt} = \sqrt{C_d W / (C_1 P)}$
  - Annual capacity: $Q = 365 \times N_b \times P_b \times S_b \times U$
  - Berth occupancy: $B_o = \Sigma T_t / (N_b \times T_{period})$
  - Crane rate: $MPH = N_{moves} / (T_{gross} \times \eta)$
- **Safety hierarchy:** Engineering controls → Administrative controls → PPE → Emergency response.
- **Always state units** in numerical answers.
- **Always round up** $n_{opt}$ to the next whole integer.
- **Always verify** the optimum by computing cost at $n-1$, $n$, and $n+1$.
- **Standard industry values:** STS crane = **30–40 MPH**, grab crane = **300–500 TPH**, demurrage = **$25k–$150k/day**.
- **Codes to remember:** IMDG, SOLAS, CSS Code, ISGOTT, OSHA 29 CFR 1917 (Marine Terminals).
- **Cargo securing principles:** Lashing must withstand **acceleration forces of up to 2g** in any direction.
- **Hatch cover test:** Watertight integrity tested by **hose test** or **ultrasonic test** before sailing.
- **The ship-shore interface** is the **single most critical safety zone** — highest accident risk density.
- **Berth occupancy limits:** < 60% = uncongested, 60–80% = optimal, > 80% = congested (port needs expansion).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->
