# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define a grease rack and list any four types commonly used in transportation workshops.

**[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model Question]**

**Model Answer:**

A **grease rack** is a dedicated service bay in a vehicle maintenance workshop designed to provide ergonomic access to the underside of a vehicle for routine lubrication, oil change, and undercarriage inspection operations.

**Four types of grease racks:**

1. **In-Ground Pit Type** — A trench dug into the workshop floor (1.2 m–1.5 m deep) into which the technician descends. **(3 marks for definition + types)**

---

### Question 2: What is a zerk fitting? State its function in grease rack operation.

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model Question]**

**Model Answer:**

A **zerk fitting** (also called an **Alemite fitting** or **grease nipple**) is a small, standardized metal connector installed at chassis lubrication points on a vehicle. It has a spring-loaded check ball that opens when a grease gun nozzle is pressed against it, allowing grease to be injected into the joint or bearing.

**Function:** It provides a **leak-proof, reusable, and standardized connection point** for injecting lubricating grease into chassis components such as ball joints, tie-rod ends, control arm bushings, and universal joints. **(3 marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)

**Q. (a)** Explain in detail the **step-by-step procedure for chassis lubrication** at a grease rack station. List the standard tools and materials required. **(7 marks)**

**Q. (b)** A pneumatic grease gun has an air piston diameter of **75 mm** and a grease piston diameter of **10 mm**. The shop air supply is regulated to **6.5 bar**. If the mechanical efficiency is **88%**, calculate:
1. The intensification ratio
2. The theoretical output grease pressure
3. The actual output pressure
4. The output force at the grease piston

**(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Q. (a) — 7 Marks:**

**Chassis Lubrication Procedure (12 Steps — covered in Section 2.2):**

For KTU board answers, present the **key lubrication steps** in tabular or bulleted form. The examiner expects the following 6 critical steps for full marks:

1. **Pre-service preparation** — review job card, identify vehicle, gather chassis lubrication chart
2. **Vehicle positioning and safety** — drive onto rack, engage parking brake, chock wheels
3. **Zerk identification** — locate all grease points using service manual (typically 25–40 points)
4. **Cleaning** — wipe each zerk fitting with clean rag to prevent dirt ingress
5. **Grease application** — attach gun, pump until fresh grease appears at joint seal (2–6 mL per point)
6. **Excess removal and inspection** — wipe excess, inspect for leaks, record on job card

**Standard Tools Required (any 6 for full marks):**
- Pneumatic grease gun with hose reel
- Hand grease gun (backup)
- Zerk fitting cleaning brush
- Used oil drain pan (8–12 L)
- Oil filter wrench
- Set of combination wrenches
- Service manual for vehicle make/model
- Wheel chocks
- Personal protective equipment (gloves, safety glasses, steel-toe boots)
- Fire extinguisher (Class B)

**(Full 7 marks awarded for covering all 6 procedure steps + at least 6 tools listed with units/sizes)**

---

**Model Solution for Q. (b) — 7 Marks:**

**Given:**
- $d_{air} = 75 \text{ mm} = 0.075 \text{ m}$
- $d_{grease} = 10 \text{ mm} = 0.010 \text{ m}$
- $P_{air} = 6.5 \text{ bar} = 6.5 \times 10^5 \text{ Pa}$
- $\eta_{mech} = 0.88$

**Part 1 — Intensification ratio:**

$$A_{air} = \frac{\pi}{4} \times (0.075)^2 = 4.418 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2$$

$$A_{grease} = \frac{\pi}{4} \times (0.010)^2 = 7.854 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^2$$

$$R_i = \frac{A_{air}}{A_{grease}} = \frac{4.418 \times 10^{-3}}{7.854 \times 10^{-5}} = 56.25$$

**Part 2 — Theoretical output pressure:**

$$P_{grease,th} = P_{air} \times R_i = 6.5 \times 56.25 = 365.625 \text{ bar}$$

$$P_{grease,th} = 36.56 \text{ MPa}$$

**Part 3 — Actual output pressure:**

$$P_{grease,act} = 0.88 \times 365.625 = 321.75 \text{ bar}$$

$$P_{grease,act} = 32.175 \text{ MPa}$$

**Part 4 — Output force at grease piston:**

$$F_{out} = P_{grease,act} \times A_{grease}$$

$$F_{out} = 321.75 \times 10^5 \times 7.854 \times 10^{-5}$$

$$F_{out} = 2526.9 \text{ N} \approx 257.7 \text{ kgf}$$

**Final answer box:**
$$\boxed{R_i = 56.25, \quad P_{th} = 365.6 \text{ bar}, \quad P_{act} = 321.75 \text{ bar}, \quad F_{out} = 2526.9 \text{ N}}$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**Q. (a)** Compare the four major types of grease racks (pit, drive-over surface, four-post lift, and scissor lift) under the following heads: **working principle, advantages, disadvantages, and ideal application**. **(7 marks)**

**Q. (b)** A commercial vehicle service center services **75 vehicles per day**. Each vehicle has on average **40 grease points**, and each point requires **5 mL of grease** (density **0.92 g/mL**). Calculate:
1. Total daily grease consumption in kg
2. Monthly consumption (26 working days)
3. Monthly procurement cost if grease costs ₹520 per kg
4. Number of grease rack bays required if each bay completes one vehicle in 30 minutes and the center operates 9 hours per day

**(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Q. (a) — 7 Marks:**

Present as a **comparison table** (this is the format KTU examiners prefer for full marks):

| Feature | Pit Type | Drive-Over Surface | Four-Post Lift | Scissor Lift |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Principle** | Vehicle drives over trench; technician enters pit | Vehicle drives onto raised platform | Vehicle lifted by four hydraulic posts | Vehicle lifted by crossed-arm hydraulics |
| **Advantage** | Stable, full access, fast | No excavation, portable | Adjustable height, versatile | Compact, low cost |
| **Disadvantage** | Confined-space hazard | Not for low cars | High cost | Less stable at height |
| **Ideal Use** | High-volume fleet workshops | Budget small workshops | Multi-brand service centers | Quick-lube stations |

**(1.75 marks per row × 4 rows = 7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Q. (b) — 7 Marks:**

**Part 1 — Daily grease consumption:**

$$C_{daily} = N_{cars} \times N_{points} \times \overline{V}_{point} \times \rho_{grease}$$

$$C_{daily} = 75 \times 40 \times 5 \times 0.92$$

$$C_{daily} = 13,800 \text{ g} = 13.8 \text{ kg/day}$$

**Part 2 — Monthly consumption:**

$$C_{monthly} = 13.8 \times 26 = 358.8 \text{ kg/month}$$

**Part 3 — Monthly cost:**

$$\text{Cost} = 358.8 \times 520 = \text{₹}186,576 \text{ per month}$$

**Part 4 — Number of bays required:**

Vehicles per bay per day:

$$n_{bay} = \frac{H_{op} \times 60}{T_{cycle}} = \frac{9 \times 60}{30} = 18 \text{ vehicles/day/bay}$$

Number of bays:

$$N_{bays} = \frac{75}{18} = 4.17 \Rightarrow \text{round up to } 5 \text{ bays}$$

**Final answer box:**
$$\boxed{C_{daily} = 13.8 \text{ kg}, \quad C_{monthly} = 358.8 \text{ kg}, \quad \text{Cost} = \text{₹}186,576, \quad N_{bays} = 5}$$

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistakes students make in grease rack questions (leading to 2–4 mark deductions):**
>
> 1. **Forgetting units in the final answer** — Pressure without bar/MPa is incomplete. Always write the unit.
> 2. **Using $\pi$ as 3.14 inconsistently** — Show $\pi$ symbolically in intermediate steps; substitute at the end.
> 3. **Mixing up diameter and radius** — The formula uses diameter squared. A radius error gives a 4× wrong answer.
> 4. **Ignoring mechanical efficiency** — The question explicitly states efficiency; omitting it loses 1.5 marks.
> 5. **In safety questions, listing hazards without control measures** — Examiners expect both hazard and mitigation.
> 6. **In comparison questions, missing the "ideal application" column** — This is a frequent KTU requirement.
> 7. **Rounding up the bay count** — Always round UP for capacity calculations; rounding down creates a bottleneck.
> 8. **Forgetting to mention confined-space safety** for pit-type racks — This is a non-negotiable point for pit-type answers.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for Grease Rack Operation (KTU Module 4):**

### Core Concepts
- Grease rack = dedicated service bay for undercarriage maintenance
- Three primary access methods: **pit, surface ramp, lift**
- Zerk fitting = standardized grease injection point
- Chassis lubrication interval: **6 months or 10,000 km**, whichever is earlier
- Grease consumption per car: **80 g to 180 g**
- Pit depth: **1.2 m – 1.5 m**
- Shop air pressure: **6–8 bar**; output grease pressure: **30–70 MPa**
- Drain time for engine oil: **minimum 8–12 minutes**

### Key Formulas
- $P_{grease} = P_{air} \times R_i \times \eta_{mech}$
- $R_i = (d_{air}/d_{grease})^2$
- $C_{daily} = N_{cars} \times N_{points} \times \overline{V} \times \rho$
- $N_{bays} = V_{daily} \times T_{cycle} / (H_{op} \times 60)$
- Approach angle $\theta = \tan^{-1}(H/L) \leq 12°$

### Safety Must-Knows
- Pit-type racks over 1.2 m are **OSHA-classified confined spaces**
- Fire extinguisher: **Class B** (flammable liquids)
- Wheel chocks mandatory on **at least two wheels**
- Pit floor drainage slope: **1 in 40 to 1 in 60**
- Ventilation is **critical** for pit-type racks

### Standards Referenced
- **IS 8264:2004** — Motor Vehicle Service Station Code of Practice
- **IS 3646** — Code of Practice for Interior Illumination
- **OSHA 1910.177** — Motor Vehicle Service Operations
- **OSHA 1910.146** — Permit-Required Confined Spaces

### Exam Pattern Reminders
- Part A (3 marks): Definition or short factual question
- Part B (14 marks): Choice between two questions, each with two 7-mark sub-parts
- Always include **units** in numerical answers
- Always state **assumptions** clearly (efficiency = 100% unless stated)
- For comparison questions, **tabular format** scores higher marks
- For procedure questions, list steps in **chronological order** with **time/duration** specifications

> **Final Tip:** Grease rack operation is a **practical, applied topic** in KTU Module 4. Examiners reward students who can **connect theory to workshop practice**. Always mention the "why" behind each step — for example, "warm the engine for 3–5 minutes **to reduce oil viscosity** for complete drainage." This demonstrates engineering understanding, not just rote memorization.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Wash Rack Operation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT527 - TRANSPORTATION SYSTEMS AND SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Wash Rack Operation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Wash Rack Operation** is a systematically engineered cleaning and maintenance facility activity conducted in transportation depots, fleet yards, and terminal facilities, wherein commercial vehicles (trucks, buses, trailers, tankers) are positioned over a reinforced drainage platform and subjected to a sequence of high-pressure water jets, detergent application, scrubbing, rinsing, and drying procedures, while ensuring compliance with safety, environmental, and operational protocols.

In the context of **PESFT527 - Transportation Systems and Safety**, the wash rack is not merely a cleaning station but a **critical operational subsystem** that intersects with vehicle safety, environmental management, and preventive maintenance scheduling.

### 1.1.1 Constituent Elements of a Wash Rack Facility

| Component | Engineering Function |
|---|---|
| **Drainage Platform** | Reinforced concrete slab with proper slope (1:60 to 1:100) for water collection |
| **High-Pressure System** | Delivers water at **80–200 bar** pressure for contaminant removal |
| **Detergent Dispenser** | Automated chemical mixing at controlled concentrations (typically **2–5%**) |
| **Oil-Water Separator** | Primary treatment unit for effluent management |
| **Safety Barriers** | Physical demarcation preventing unauthorized access during operation |
| **Lighting Array** | Minimum **200 lux** illumination for night operations |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Think of a wash rack as a "vehicle spa with engineering discipline."**
>
> Imagine a car wash you visit personally — but scaled up massively, with industrial-grade equipment, strict safety protocols, and environmental safeguards. Just as a spa follows specific steps (cleansing, exfoliation, rinsing, moisturizing), a wash rack follows a precise **operational sequence** for heavy commercial vehicles. The difference is that in transportation engineering, the wash rack serves three critical purposes simultaneously: **cleanliness for aesthetics/inspection, safety for hazard prevention, and environmental compliance for legal adherence**.

## 1.3 Why Wash Rack Operations Matter in Transportation Safety

> [!NOTE]
> **Safety Implication:** A dirty vehicle is not just an aesthetic problem — accumulated dirt, grease, and chemical residue can:
> - Obscure critical safety components (brake lights, reflectors, windshield)
> - Mask structural damage during visual inspections
> - Create slip hazards for operators during pre-trip inspections
> - Contribute to corrosion that compromises structural integrity
> - Lead to **non-compliance penalties** under motor vehicle regulations

### 1.3.1 Frequency Standards in Commercial Fleets

- **Daily Wash**: City buses, passenger vehicles (high visibility requirements)
- **Weekly Wash**: Long-haul trucks, distribution vehicles
- **Trip-Based Wash**: Tanker vehicles carrying hazardous materials (regulatory mandate)
- **Pre-Inspection Wash**: Before scheduled maintenance or annual fitness certification
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Classification of Wash Racks

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU students must distinguish between the **four primary classifications** of wash rack systems, as examination questions frequently test this differentiation.

### 2.1.1 Classification by Automation Level

**Type 1 — Manual Wash Rack:**
- Operator-driven with handheld pressure washers
- Suitable for **small fleets (< 10 vehicles)**
- Labor-intensive but flexible for varied vehicle geometries

**Type 2 — Semi-Automatic Wash Rack:**
- Fixed overhead gantry with manually operated side nozzles
- Operator controls wash sequence from a **centralized control panel**
- Common in **medium-scale fleet operations (10–50 vehicles)**

**Type 3 — Fully Automatic Drive-Through Wash Rack:**
- Sensor-activated brush and jet systems
- Vehicle pulled through on a **controlled conveyor or guided track**
- Water consumption: **80–150 liters per vehicle**

**Type 4 — Robotic/Touchless Wash Rack:**
- Uses **high-pressure water jets only (no brushes)**
- Eliminates paint damage risk
- Preferred for **premium fleet and tanker operations**

### 2.1.2 Classification by Mobility

- **Fixed Wash Rack**: Permanent installation at depot
- **Portable Wash Rack**: Trailer-mounted units for remote site operations
- **Mobile Wash Rack Truck**: Self-propelled units for field deployment

## 2.2 Standard Operating Procedure (SOP) — Step-by-Step Logic

> [!NOTE]
> The following sequence represents the **industry-standard SOP** that KTU examiners expect students to articulate in descriptive questions.

**Step 1 — Pre-Operation Safety Check**
- Verify all emergency stop systems functional
- Check PPE availability for operators (gloves, goggles, non-slip boots)
- Confirm drainage system is unobstructed

**Step 2 — Vehicle Positioning**
- Vehicle aligned on drainage platform with proper centering
- Engine OFF, parking brake engaged, wheels chocked
- All electrical systems (if EV/hybrid) secured

**Step 3 — Pre-Wash Rinse**
- Low-pressure rinse to remove loose debris
- Prevents scratching during detergent application
- Typical pressure: **3–5 bar**

**Step 4 — Detergent Application**
- Alkaline or pH-neutral detergent applied via foam system
- Contact time: **3–5 minutes** for effective soil suspension
- Coverage verified for undercarriage and wheel wells

**Step 5 — High-Pressure Wash**
- Systematic top-to-bottom cleaning sequence
- Pressure range: **80–200 bar** depending on surface
- Distance maintained: **30–60 cm** from surface

**Step 6 — Final Rinse**
- Deionized or softened water to prevent spotting
- Pressure: **5–10 bar**

**Step 7 — Drying Phase**
- Forced air drying or chamois wipe-down
- Critical for preventing water ingress in electrical components

**Step 8 — Post-Wash Inspection**
- Visual safety check (lights, reflectors, markings)
- Documentation in fleet management system

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Parameter | Formula / Standard Value | Units |
|---|---|---|
| **Water Consumption per Vehicle** | $Q_w = A_v \times R_c$ where $A_v$ = vehicle surface area, $R_c$ = rinse coefficient | Liters |
| **Drainage Slope Requirement** | $S_d \geq \frac{1}{60}$ (IRC recommended minimum) | Ratio |
| **Detergent Concentration** | $C_d = \frac{V_{chem}}{V_{total}} \times 100$ | Percentage (%) |
| **Wash Cycle Time** | $T_c = T_{pos} + T_{rinse} + T_{det} + T_{wash} + T_{frinse} + T_{dry}$ | Minutes |
| **Oil-Water Separator Efficiency** | $\eta_{ows} = \frac{C_{in} - C_{out}}{C_{in}} \times 100$ | Percentage (%) |
| **Effective Pressure at Nozzle** | $P_e = P_s - \Delta P_{friction}$ | Bar |
| **Flow Rate Through Nozzle** | $Q = C_d \cdot A_n \cdot \sqrt{\frac{2 \Delta P}{\rho}}$ | Liters/second |
| **Undercarriage Wash Pressure** | $P_{uc} \geq 100$ bar (heavy vehicles) | Bar |
| **PPE Slip Resistance Rating** | $\mu \geq 0.5$ (coefficient of friction) | Dimensionless |
| **Effluent pH Compliance** | $6.5 \leq pH \leq 9.0$ (KSPCB standard) | pH units |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Industry Application Context:** Wash rack operations are integral to:
> - **Public Transport Depots (KSRTC, BMTC)**: Daily bus cleaning affects passenger perception and corrosion prevention
> - **Logistics Hubs (Amazon, Flipkart)**: Fleet turnaround efficiency directly impacts delivery SLAs
> - **Hazardous Material Transport**: Regulatory mandate under **ADR (UN)** and **Central Motor Vehicle Rules (CMVR), India**
> - **Port Operations**: Container trucks and trailers require salt-removal washing to prevent accelerated corrosion in marine environments

### 2.4.1 Safety Engineering Relevance

The wash rack serves as a **secondary inspection point** where operators can identify:
- Cracked windshields
- Damaged reflectors and conspicuity tape
- Loose body panels
- Fluid leaks (oil, coolant, brake fluid)
- Tire sidewall damage

This **dual function** (cleaning + safety screening) makes wash rack operations a critical node in the **transportation safety management system (TSMS)**.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Optimum Wash Rack Throughput Calculation

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Context:** A bus depot operates 12 hours daily with a semi-automatic wash rack. Determine the maximum fleet capacity the facility can service.

### 3.1.1 Given Parameters

- Total operational time: $T_{total} = 12$ hours = **720 minutes**
- Average wash cycle time per vehicle: $T_c = 25$ minutes
- Setup/changeover time between vehicles: $T_s = 5$ minutes
- Maintenance downtime allowance: $T_m = 60$ minutes/day
- Peak demand surge factor: $K_s = 1.15$

### 3.1.2 Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1: Calculate Effective Operational Time**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{eff} &= T_{total} - T_m \\
T_{eff} &= 720 - 60 \\
T_{eff} &= 660 \text{ minutes}
\end{aligned}
$$

This represents the actual time available for productive washing after maintenance windows.

**Step 2: Calculate Effective Cycle Time per Vehicle**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{cycle} &= T_c + T_s \\
T_{cycle} &= 25 + 5 \\
T_{cycle} &= 30 \text{ minutes per vehicle}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Calculate Base Throughput Capacity**

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{base} &= \frac{T_{eff}}{T_{cycle}} \\
N_{base} &= \frac{660}{30} \\
N_{base} &= 22 \text{ vehicles/day}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Apply Surge Factor for Peak Demand**

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{peak} &= N_{base} \times K_s \\
N_{peak} &= 22 \times 1.15 \\
N_{peak} &= 25.3 \\
N_{peak} &\approx 25 \text{ vehicles/day}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5: Engineering Conclusion**

The wash rack can service a **maximum fleet of 22 vehicles** under normal conditions, with a **surge capacity of 25 vehicles/day** during peak demand periods.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Detergent Dilution Calculation

### 3.2.1 Problem Setup

A wash rack uses a concentrated detergent (15% active ingredient) that must be diluted to a working concentration of **3%** for safe vehicle washing. The mixing tank capacity is **500 liters**.

**Step 1: Define Variables**

- $C_c$ = Concentration of concentrate = 15%
- $C_w$ = Required working concentration = 3%
- $V_t$ = Total volume of working solution = 500 L
- $V_c$ = Volume of concentrate required
- $V_w$ = Volume of water required

**Step 2: Apply Dilution Mass Balance**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_c \times C_c &= V_t \times C_w \\
V_c \times 15 &= 500 \times 3 \\
V_c &= \frac{1500}{15} \\
V_c &= 100 \text{ liters of concentrate}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Calculate Water Volume**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_w &= V_t - V_c \\
V_w &= 500 - 100 \\
V_w &= 400 \text{ liters of water}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Verification**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{verify} &= \frac{V_c \times C_c}{V_t} \times 100 \\
C_{verify} &= \frac{100 \times 15}{500} \times 100 \\
C_{verify} &= 3\% \quad \checkmark
\end{aligned}
$$

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Comparative Wash Rack Systems

| Parameter | Manual | Semi-Auto | Fully Auto | Robotic |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Capital Cost (INR Lakhs)** | 5–10 | 25–50 | 80–150 | 150–300 |
| **Water per Vehicle (L)** | 200–300 | 120–180 | 80–150 | 60–100 |
| **Cycle Time (min)** | 45–60 | 25–35 | 8–12 | 5–8 |
| **Labor Required** | 3–4 operators | 1–2 operators | 1 supervisor | 1 supervisor |
| **Daily Throughput** | 15–20 | 40–60 | 100–150 | 180–250 |
| **Surface Damage Risk** | Medium | Low | Medium (brushes) | Negligible |
| **Environmental Compliance** | Manual monitoring | Semi-automated | Fully monitored | Fully monitored |
| **Suitability** | Small fleet | Medium fleet | Large fleet | Premium/large fleet |

## 3.4 Safety Risk Assessment Matrix for Wash Rack Operations

| Hazard | Likelihood | Severity | Risk Score | Mitigation |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Slip and fall (wet surface)** | High (4) | Medium (3) | 12 | Anti-slip coating, drainage design |
| **Chemical exposure (detergent)** | Medium (3) | Medium (3) | 9 | PPE, MSDS training |
| **Vehicle movement during wash** | Low (2) | High (4) | 8 | Wheel chocks, brake verification |
| **Electrical hazard (water + power)** | Low (2) | High (4) | 8 | IP65 rated equipment, GFCI |
| **High-pressure injection injury** | Low (2) | High (4) | 8 | Nozzle guards, training |
| **Effluent contamination** | Medium (3) | Medium (3) | 9 | Oil-water separator, periodic testing |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Risk Score Interpretation:** Score = Likelihood × Severity (scale 1–5). Scores above 8 require **mandatory engineering controls**; scores 4–8 require administrative controls; below 4 is acceptable with monitoring.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Wash Rack Operational Flow Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    node1["VEHICLE ARRIVES AT DEPOT"] --> node2["PRE-WASH INSPECTION"]
    node2 --> node3{"VEHICLE TYPE?"}
    node3 -->|HazMat Tanker| node4A["MANDATORY DECONTAMINATION WASH"]
    node3 -->|Standard Vehicle| node4B["ROUTINE CLEANING"]
    node4A --> node5["POSITION ON WASH RACK"]
    node4B --> node5
    node5 --> node6["WHEEL CHOCKING AND BRAKE VERIFICATION"]
    node6 --> node7["PRE-RINSE LOW PRESSURE 3-5 BAR"]
    node7 --> node8["DETERGENT FOAM APPLICATION"]
    node8 --> node9{"DWELL TIME 3-5 MIN ELAPSED?"}
    node9 -->|No| node9
    node9 -->|Yes| node10["HIGH PRESSURE WASH 80-200 BAR"]
    node10 --> node11["FINAL RINSE DEIONIZED WATER"]
    node11 --> node12["FORCED AIR DRYING"]
    node12 --> node13["POST-WASH SAFETY INSPECTION"]

    node14 -->|No| node15["ROUTE TO MAINTENANCE BAY"]
    node14 -->|Yes| node16["DOCUMENT AND RELEASE VEHICLE"]
    node15 --> node17["MAINTENANCE COMPLETION"]
    node17 --> node16
    node16 --> node18["VEHICLE DEPLOYED TO SERVICE"]
```

## 4.2 Wash Rack Facility Layout — Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR

        nodeA1["ENTRY GATE"] --> nodeA2["PRE-WASH INSPECTION BAY"]
    end

    end

        nodeB1 --> nodeC1["OIL WATER SEPARATOR"]

    end
    subgraph EGRESS ["EGRESS ZONE"]

        nodeS1["EMERGENCY STOP NETWORK"] -.-> nodeB1
        nodeS2["FIRE SUPPRESSION"] -.-> nodeB1

        nodeS4["FIRST AID KIT"] -.-> nodeB1
    end
```

## 4.3 Wash Rack Safety Control Loop

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    ctl1["OPERATOR INITIATES WASH CYCLE"] --> ctl2["SYSTEM CHECK: PUMPS, NOZZLES, DRAINAGE"]
    ctl2 --> ctl3{"ALL SYSTEMS OPERATIONAL?"}
    ctl3 -->|No| ctl4["ALERT MAINTENANCE TEAM"]
    ctl3 -->|Yes| ctl5["BEGIN WASH SEQUENCE"]
    ctl5 --> ctl6["CONTINUOUS MONITORING: PRESSURE, FLOW, pH"]
    ctl6 --> ctl7{"PARAMETER DEVIATION DETECTED?"}
    ctl7 -->|Yes| ctl8["AUTOMATIC SHUTDOWN ACTIVATED"]
    ctl7 -->|No| ctl9["CONTINUE WASH CYCLE"]
    ctl9 --> ctl10["CYCLE COMPLETION LOGGED"]
    ctl8 --> ctl11["ROOT CAUSE ANALYSIS"]
    ctl11 --> ctl12["CORRECTIVE ACTION IMPLEMENTED"]
    ctl12 --> ctl2
    ctl10 --> ctl13["RELEASE VEHICLE"]
```

## 4.4 Effluent Management Process Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    eff1["WASH WATER COLLECTED ON DRAINAGE PLATFORM"] --> eff2["GRIT CHAMBER: REMOVES LARGE DEBRIS"]
    eff2 --> eff3["OIL WATER SEPARATOR: REMOVES HYDROCARBONS"]
    eff3 --> eff4{"EFFLUENT pH WITHIN 6.5-9.0?"}
    eff4 -->|No| eff5["CHEMICAL pH ADJUSTMENT TANK"]
    eff5 --> eff4
    eff4 -->|Yes| eff6["SUSPENDED SOLIDS REMOVAL FILTER"]
    eff6 --> eff7{"MEETS KSPCB DISCHARGE NORMS?"}
    eff7 -->|No| eff8["RECYCLE TO TREATMENT"]
    eff7 -->|Yes| eff9["DISCHARGE TO APPROVED SEWER LINE"]
    eff8 --> eff3
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Conceptual Definition
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**Q: Define "Wash Rack Operation" in the context of transportation systems. List its three primary engineering objectives.**

**Model Answer:**

Wash rack operation refers to the **systematic cleaning and maintenance procedure** conducted at a designated facility within a transportation depot, wherein commercial vehicles are positioned over a drainage platform and subjected to controlled water jets, detergents, and drying sequences.

The three primary engineering objectives are:

1. **Vehicle Cleanliness**: Removal of dirt, grease, road salt, and contaminants to maintain aesthetic standards and prevent corrosion.

2. **Safety Enhancement**: Facilitating visual inspection of critical safety components (lights, reflectors, structural elements) that may be obscured by accumulated grime.

3. **Environmental Compliance**: Ensuring wash water effluent is treated through oil-water separators and pH neutralization before discharge, complying with **State Pollution Control Board (KSPCB) norms** and **Central Motor Vehicle Rules**.

---

### Question 2: Short Answer
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**Q: List any six safety precautions that must be observed during wash rack operations.**

**Model Answer:**

1. Operators must wear **PPE** including non-slip boots, chemical-resistant gloves, and safety goggles.
2. Vehicle **parking brake must be engaged** and **wheels chocked** before wash initiation.
3. All **electrical equipment** in the wash zone must be **IP65 rated or higher** to prevent water ingress.
4. **Emergency stop buttons** must be accessible at multiple points within the wash bay.
5. **High-pressure hoses** must be inspected for cracks or wear before each shift to prevent injection injuries.
6. The **drainage system** must be verified clear of obstructions to prevent water pooling and slip hazards.
7. **Detergent MSDS (Material Safety Data Sheets)** must be available at the chemical storage point.
8. No personnel should be inside the vehicle cabin during automated wash cycles.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 — Model Question Paper]**

**Q: As a transportation safety engineer, you are tasked with designing the wash rack facility for a new bus depot operating 80 city buses on daily rotation.**

**(a)** Explain in detail the **standard operating procedure (SOP)** for wash rack operations in this depot, covering all eight sequential stages. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** Calculate the **daily throughput capacity** of the wash rack given the following parameters, and recommend whether **one or two wash bays** are required. **[7 Marks]**

**Given Data:**
- Daily operational hours: 16 hours (split shift)
- Wash cycle time per bus: 30 minutes
- Changeover time: 5 minutes
- Maintenance downtime: 90 minutes/day
- Peak demand surge factor: 1.20
- Target: All 80 buses must be washed daily

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Detailed SOP — 8 Stages:**

**Stage 1 — Pre-Operation Safety Check:**
- Conduct daily inspection of all wash rack equipment (pumps, nozzles, electrical systems)
- Verify emergency stop functionality at **minimum 4 locations**
- Confirm PPE inventory (gloves, goggles, non-slip boots) is complete
- Check that oil-water separator is operational
- Review **MSDS** for all chemicals on site

**Stage 2 — Vehicle Positioning:**
- Bus driven onto drainage platform at **speed < 5 km/h**
- Driver ensures proper alignment with wash gantry
- Engine switched OFF, parking brake engaged
- Wheels chocked at front and rear
- Driver and all personnel exit the vehicle

**Stage 3 — Pre-Wash Rinse:**
- Low-pressure water application (3–5 bar) from perimeter nozzles
- Removes loose dirt, leaves, and road debris
- Prevents scratching during subsequent detergent application
- Duration: **2 minutes**

**Stage 4 — Detergent Application:**
- Alkaline detergent (pH 9–11) applied via foam lance
- Coverage: full vehicle exterior, undercarriage, wheel wells
- Dwell time: **4–5 minutes** for soil suspension
- Critical for removing traffic film and grease deposits

**Stage 5 — High-Pressure Wash:**
- Pressure: **100–150 bar** for bus body
- **150–200 bar** for undercarriage (corrosion-prone zone)
- Systematic pattern: roof → sides → front → rear → undercarriage
- Nozzle distance: 30–60 cm from surface
- Duration: **8–10 minutes**

**Stage 6 — Final Rinse:**
- Deionized water to prevent mineral spotting
- Pressure: 5–10 bar
- Ensures complete detergent removal
- Duration: **2 minutes**

**Stage 7 — Drying:**
- Forced air blowers or microfiber chamois
- Critical for windows, mirrors, and electrical fittings
- Prevents water ingress into door mechanisms

**Stage 8 — Post-Wash Inspection:**
- Visual check of all safety equipment (lights, reflectors, emergency exits)
- Document wash completion in fleet management system
- Bus cleared for next-day service deployment

---

**(b) Throughput Calculation:**

**Step 1: Effective Operational Time**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{eff} &= (16 \times 60) - 90 \\
T_{eff} &= 960 - 90 \\
T_{eff} &= 870 \text{ minutes}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Effective Cycle Time**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{cycle} &= 30 + 5 = 35 \text{ minutes/bus}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Base Throughput (Single Bay)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{base} &= \frac{870}{35} \\
N_{base} &= 24.86 \\
N_{base} &\approx 24 \text{ buses/day}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Peak Capacity (Single Bay)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{peak} &= 24 \times 1.20 = 28.8 \approx 28 \text{ buses/day}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5: Decision Analysis**

Since the target is **80 buses/day** and a single bay can handle only **24–28 buses/day**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{required} &= 80 \text{ buses/day} \\
N_{available} &= 28 \text{ buses/day (single bay, peak)} \\
N_{gap} &= 80 - 28 = 52 \text{ buses/day}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Number of bays required:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
n_{bays} &= \frac{N_{required}}{N_{peak}} = \frac{80}{28.8} \\
n_{bays} &= 2.78 \\
n_{bays} &\approx 3 \text{ wash bays}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion:** A minimum of **3 wash bays** is required to meet the daily target of 80 buses, with adequate surge capacity. If budget constraints limit to 2 bays, an extended operational window or shift adjustment would be necessary.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 — Model Question Paper]**

**Q: (a)** Describe the **four classifications of wash rack systems** based on automation level, with their respective advantages and limitations. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** Discuss the **environmental compliance requirements** for wash rack effluent discharge, including the role of the oil-water separator and pH neutralization system. **[7 Marks]**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Four Classifications of Wash Rack Systems:**

**1. Manual Wash Rack:**
- **Mechanism:** Operator uses handheld pressure washers and brushes
- **Advantages:** Low capital cost (INR 5–10 lakhs), flexible for varied vehicle shapes, suitable for spot cleaning
- **Limitations:** High water consumption (200–300 L/vehicle), labor-intensive, inconsistent quality, longer cycle time (45–60 min)

**2. Semi-Automatic Wash Rack:**
- **Mechanism:** Fixed overhead gantry with manually positioned side nozzles
- **Advantages:** Moderate cost (INR 25–50 lakhs), 1–2 operators sufficient, good quality control
- **Limitations:** Still requires operator intervention, moderate water use (120–180 L/vehicle), 25–35 min cycle

**3. Fully Automatic Drive-Through Wash Rack:**
- **Mechanism:** Vehicle pulled through on conveyor; sensor-activated brush and jet systems
- **Advantages:** High throughput (100–150 vehicles/day), consistent quality, lower labor cost
- **Limitations:** High capital cost (INR 80–150 lakhs), brush damage risk to vehicle paint, less flexible for non-standard vehicles

**4. Robotic/Touchless Wash Rack:**
- **Mechanism:** High-pressure water jets (no brushes), programmable robotic arms
- **Advantages:** Zero surface damage, lowest water use (60–100 L/vehicle), highest throughput
- **Limitations:** Highest capital cost (INR 150–300 lakhs), sophisticated maintenance, requires skilled technical staff

---

**(b) Environmental Compliance for Effluent Discharge:**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Wash rack effluent contains **hydrocarbons (oil, grease), suspended solids, detergents, and pH-altering chemicals**. Direct discharge to municipal drains is prohibited under the **Water (Prevention and Control of Pollution) Act, 1974** and KSPCB regulations.

**Key Compliance Parameters:**

**1. Oil-Water Separator (OWS):**
- **Function:** Uses gravity differential to separate hydrocarbons from water
- **Design:** Typically API separator or coalescing plate type
- **Efficiency Requirement:** $\eta_{ows} \geq 95\%$ for oil removal
- **Maintenance:** Weekly sludge removal, quarterly efficiency testing

**2. pH Neutralization System:**
- **Function:** Adjusts acidic/alkaline effluent to neutral range
- **Acceptable Range:** **6.5 ≤ pH ≤ 9.0** (KSPCB standard)
- **Mechanism:** Automated dosing of acid (H₂SO₄) or base (NaOH) based on real-time pH sensor feedback
- **Critical for:** Preventing damage to municipal sewer infrastructure and aquatic ecosystems

**3. Suspended Solids Management:**
- **Filtration Stage:** Sand filters or membrane systems
- **Maximum Permissible:** Total Suspended Solids (TSS) < 100 mg/L

**4. Regulatory Documentation:**
- Monthly effluent testing reports submitted to KSPCB
- Consent to Operate (CTO) renewal every 5 years
- Records of chemical inventory and consumption

**5. Water Recycling (Best Practice):**
- Treated effluent recycled for pre-wash rinsing
- Reduces freshwater consumption by up to **60%**
- Aligns with **National Mission on Sustainable Habitat** objectives

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
>
> 1. **Students frequently confuse "wash rack" with "car wash"** — remember, in KTU's transportation engineering context, the emphasis is on **commercial heavy vehicles, safety integration, and environmental compliance**, not passenger cars.
>
> 2. **Numerical calculation errors** in throughput problems are common — always show unit conversions explicitly (hours to minutes) and label each variable clearly.
>
> 3. **Omitting the environmental compliance component** in descriptive answers leads to significant mark loss — every wash rack answer in PESFT527 must reference effluent management, since it falls under Transportation Systems AND Safety.
>
> 4. **Neglecting PPE and operator safety** is a frequent oversight — examiners specifically look for safety integration in operational descriptions.
>
> 5. **Forgetting the post-wash inspection function** — wash racks are not just cleaning stations; they serve as **secondary safety screening points** for vehicle roadworthiness.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Wash Rack Operation**

### Core Concepts
- ☐ Wash rack = engineered cleaning facility with **drainage, water supply, chemical dispensing, and safety systems**
- ☐ Four types: **Manual, Semi-Automatic, Fully Automatic, Robotic/Touchless**
- ☐ Standard SOP has **8 sequential stages** (pre-check → positioning → pre-rinse → detergent → high-pressure wash → final rinse → drying → inspection)

### Critical Numerical Values
- ☐ **Pre-wash pressure:** 3–5 bar
- ☐ **High-pressure wash:** 80–200 bar
- ☐ **Final rinse:** 5–10 bar
- ☐ **Detergent dwell time:** 3–5 minutes
- ☐ **Effluent pH compliance:** 6.5–9.0
- ☐ **Drainage slope:** ≥ 1:60
- ☐ **Oil-water separator efficiency:** ≥ 95%

### Safety Integration Points
- ☐ Wheel chocking is **mandatory** before wash initiation
- ☐ Emergency stop buttons at **multiple locations** in wash bay
- ☐ All electrical equipment must be **IP65 rated minimum**
- ☐ Operator PPE: non-slip boots, gloves, goggles
- ☐ High-pressure hose inspection **per shift**

### Environmental Compliance
- ☐ KSPCB discharge norms applicable
- ☐ Oil-water separator mandatory
- ☐ pH neutralization before sewer discharge
- ☐ Water recycling recommended (60% reduction possible)

### Examination Strategy
- ☐ Always reference **three objectives**: cleanliness, safety, environment
- ☐ Show **complete step-by-step calculations** in numerical problems
- ☐ Include **diagrams/labels** where possible (extra credit from examiners)
- ☐ Connect wash rack to **broader TSMS (Transportation Safety Management System)**

### Common Confusions to Avoid
- ☐ Wash rack ≠ car wash (commercial vehicle focus)
- ☐ High-pressure wash ≠ steam cleaning (different mechanisms)
- ☐ Oil-water separator ≠ water softener (different functions)
- ☐ Post-wash inspection ≠ fitness certification (preliminary check only)
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Battery Charging
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT527 - TRANSPORTATION SYSTEMS AND SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Battery Charging

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Battery Charging** in the context of electric vehicles (EVs) is the controlled process of restoring electrical energy to a rechargeable battery pack from an external power source, governed by a **Battery Management System (BMS)** that regulates voltage, current, temperature, and **State of Charge (SOC)** to ensure safety, longevity, and efficiency.

In transportation systems, battery charging is the **refueling equivalent** for electric vehicles, forming the operational backbone of EV infrastructure. The KTU 2024 Scheme (PESFT527) categorizes charging into:
- **Conductive Charging** (wired: AC and DC)
- **Inductive Charging** (wireless: static and dynamic)
- **Battery Swapping** (mechanical exchange of depleted packs)

The primary standard metric is the **C-rate**, which expresses charging current relative to the battery's nominal capacity.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> Think of a battery like a **water tank with a smart inlet valve**:
> - The **tank** is the battery (its capacity = tank volume in liters).
> - The **water** is the electrical energy (measured in kWh).
> - The **inlet valve** is the BMS — it opens wide when the tank is empty (high current accepted) and slowly closes as the tank fills (constant voltage phase, current tapers).
> - The **water pressure** is the voltage.
> - If you force water in too fast, the tank heats up, the walls stress, and you risk a **burst** — same as thermal runaway in a lithium-ion cell.

### 1.3 Key Physical Constants and Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Charging Metrics (Memorize for KTU):**
> - **SOC (State of Charge):** 0% (empty) to 100% (full) — dimensionless.
> - **C-rate:** $C = \dfrac{I}{Q_{cap}}$ where $I$ is current (A) and $Q_{cap}$ is rated capacity (Ah).
> - **1C** = full charge in **1 hour**; **2C** = 30 min; **0.5C** = 2 hours.
> - **Energy unit:** **1 kWh = 3.6 MJ = 1000 Wh**.
> - Standard onboard charger ratings: **3.3 kW, 6.6 kW, 11 kW, 22 kW (AC)**.
> - DC fast charger ratings: **50 kW, 150 kW, 350 kW, 400+ kW**.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Charging Levels (SAE J1772 / IEC 61851 Standard)

**Level 1 (AC Slow Charging)**
- Supply: **120 V AC (US)** / **230 V AC (EU/India)** — single phase.
- Power: **1.4 kW – 2.3 kW**.
- Current: **12 A – 16 A**.
- Use case: Home overnight charging, 8–12 hours for a 30 kWh pack.
- No dedicated infrastructure required; uses standard wall outlet.

**Level 2 (AC Fast Charging)**
- Supply: **208–240 V AC** — single or three phase.
- Power: **3.3 kW – 22 kW** (most common 7.4 kW / 11 kW).
- Current: **16 A – 80 A**.
- Use case: Public stations, workplaces, homes with EVSE.
- Full charge: **3–6 hours**.

**Level 3 (DC Fast Charging / DCFC)**
- Supply: **200–1000 V DC** (high-voltage DC bus).
- Power: **50 kW – 400+ kW**.
- Bypasses onboard charger; talks directly to BMS.
- Use case: Highway corridors, urban fast-charge hubs.
- Charging time: **15–30 minutes to 80% SOC**.

### 2.2 Charging Profiles (How a Lithium-Ion Cell Actually Charges)

A lithium-ion cell follows a **Constant Current – Constant Voltage (CC-CV)** profile:

**Phase 1 — Constant Current (CC):**
- BMS supplies a fixed current (e.g., 0.5C, 1C, or higher).
- Cell voltage rises linearly from ~3.0 V to ~4.2 V (per cell).
- ~70–80% of the capacity is filled in this phase.
- **Why:** Maximum energy transfer rate without exceeding safe voltage.

**Phase 2 — Constant Voltage (CV):**
- Voltage held at **4.2 V/cell**; current tapers exponentially.
- Final 20–30% SOC delivered here.
- **Why:** Prevents over-voltage, lithium plating, and electrolyte decomposition.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Frequently Tested Point:** DC fast charging typically stops at **80% SOC** to avoid the time-consuming CV tail. The remaining 20% may take as long as the first 80%!

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $t = \dfrac{\Delta SOC \cdot C_{pack}}{P_{chg} \cdot \eta_{chg}}$ | Charging time from SOC$_i$ to SOC$_f$ | hours |
| 2 | $E_{delivered} = V_{pack} \cdot I_{chg} \cdot t$ | Energy stored in battery | kWh |
| 3 | $C\text{-rate} = \dfrac{I_{chg}}{Q_{Ah}}$ | Charging current normalized to capacity | 1/h |
| 4 | $P_{chg} = V \cdot I \cdot \cos\phi$ | AC charging power (with power factor) | kW |
| 5 | $\eta_{chg} = \dfrac{E_{stored}}{E_{grid}} \times 100\%$ | Charging efficiency (grid-to-battery) | % |
| 6 | $I_{cell} = \dfrac{C\text{-rate} \cdot C_{cap}}{n_{parallel}}$ | Per-cell current in pack | A |
| 7 | $Q_{heat} = I^2 \cdot R_{int} \cdot t$ | Heat generated inside cell (Joule heating) | J |
| 8 | $R_{pack} = \dfrac{n_s \cdot R_{cell}}{n_p}$ | Equivalent pack resistance | $\Omega$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** These formulas are used in EV design (range estimation, charger sizing), grid planning (load forecasting for charging stations), and thermal management system design.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Charging Time for an EV Battery Pack

**Problem Setup:** A Tata Nexon EV has a **30.2 kWh** battery. The owner wants to charge from **SOC = 20%** to **SOC = 80%** using a **7.4 kW Level 2 AC charger** with overall charging efficiency **$\eta_{chg} = 0.90$**. Calculate the charging time.

**Step 1 — Identify given quantities**
- Battery capacity: $C_{pack} = 30.2$ kWh
- Initial SOC: $SOC_i = 20\% = 0.20$
- Final SOC: $SOC_f = 80\% = 0.80$
- Charger power: $P_{chg} = 7.4$ kW
- Efficiency: $\eta_{chg} = 0.90$

**Step 2 — Calculate SOC delta**

$$
\Delta SOC = SOC_f - SOC_i = 0.80 - 0.20 = 0.60
$$

**Step 3 — Compute energy to be delivered (accounting for efficiency)**

Energy drawn from the **grid** must be higher than energy **stored** in the battery, because some energy is lost as heat in the onboard charger and in cell internal resistance.

$$
E_{grid} = \dfrac{\Delta SOC \cdot C_{pack}}{\eta_{chg}}
$$

Substituting values:

$$
E_{grid} = \dfrac{0.60 \times 30.2 \text{ kWh}}{0.90} = \dfrac{18.12 \text{ kWh}}{0.90} = 20.13 \text{ kWh}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute charging time**

$$
t = \dfrac{E_{grid}}{P_{chg}} = \dfrac{20.13 \text{ kWh}}{7.4 \text{ kW}}
$$

$$
t = 2.72 \text{ hours} \approx 2 \text{ h } 43 \text{ min}
$$

**Step 5 — Verification with dimensional analysis**

$$
[t] = \dfrac{[\text{kWh}]}{[\text{kW}]} = [\text{h}] \;\;\checkmark
$$

**Step 6 — Real-world interpretation**

> [!NOTE]
> A 7.4 kW AC charger adds roughly **11 kWh per hour** of useful energy. To go from 20% to 80% on a 30 kWh pack requires ~18 kWh of useful energy → ~2 hours 43 min, which matches the calculated value.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Maximum Safe Charging Current (C-Rate Limitation)

**Problem Setup:** A NMC lithium-ion cell has capacity **$Q_{cap} = 50$ Ah** and maximum recommended charging C-rate **0.5C**. Find the maximum charging current, and the time to charge from 0% to 100% SOC.

**Step 1 — Apply C-rate definition**

$$
I_{max} = C\text{-rate} \times Q_{cap}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute values**

$$
I_{max} = 0.5 \times 50 \text{ Ah} = 25 \text{ A}
$$

**Step 3 — Time for full charge at this C-rate**

Since 1C would mean full charge in 1 hour, 0.5C means full charge in 2 hours (theoretical). Practically, with CC-CV taper, add 15–20%:

$$
t_{theoretical} = \dfrac{1}{0.5} = 2.0 \text{ h}
$$

$$
t_{actual} \approx 1.15 \times t_{theoretical} = 2.3 \text{ hours}
$$

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Charging Method Comparison

| Parameter | Level 1 (AC) | Level 2 (AC) | Level 3 (DCFC) | Battery Swap |
|-----------|--------------|--------------|----------------|--------------|
| **Power** | 1.4–2.3 kW | 3.3–22 kW | 50–400 kW | N/A |
| **Voltage** | 120/230 V AC | 208–240 V AC | 200–1000 V DC | DC bus |
| **Time (30 kWh)** | 12–18 h | 3–8 h | 20–40 min | < 5 min |
| **Connector** | Type 1/2 | Type 1/2 (Mennekes) | CCS2, CHAdeMO, GB/T | Robotic arm |
| **Onboard Charger** | Required | Required | **Bypassed** | N/A |
| **Grid Impact** | Negligible | Moderate | High (transformer needed) | High but short |
| **Typical Use** | Home | Workplace, mall | Highway corridor | Fleet, taxi |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram: AC vs DC Charging Power Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    GridAC[Grid AC Supply] -->|Single/3-Phase| EVSE[EVSE Wallbox]
    EVSE -->|Control Pilot Signal| OBC[Onboard Charger AC-DC]
    OBC -->|Rectified DC| BMS1[BMS and Pack]
    BMS1 --> CellA[Cell Module 1]
    BMS1 --> CellB[Cell Module 2]
    BMS1 --> CellC[Cell Module N]

    GridDC[Grid AC] --> Rectifier[Off-board Rectifier Station]
    Rectifier -->|High Voltage DC| CCS[CCS2 Connector]
    CCS -->|Direct to Pack| BMS2[BMS and High Voltage Bus]
    BMS2 --> PackDC[Battery Pack]
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram: CC-CV Charging Profile State Machine

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Init: SOC less than 10 percent
    Init --> CC: Precharge Complete
    CC --> CV: Voltage reaches 4.2 V per cell
    CV --> Terminate: Current less than 0.05C
    Terminate --> [*]: SOC 100 percent Balancing
    CC --> Fault: Overcurrent or Over Temp
    CV --> Fault: Overvoltage detected
    Fault --> [*]: BMS Disconnect
```

### 4.3 Block Diagram: Smart Charging and V2G Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Cloud[Cloud Platform OCPP Backend] -->|Schedule, Pricing| Charger[Smart Charger]
    Charger -->|PWM Pilot| EV[Electric Vehicle]
    EV <-->|Bidirectional Power| Charger
    Charger <-->|V2G Reverse Power Flow| Grid[Local Microgrid]
    Grid --> PV[Solar PV]
    Grid --> BESS[Stationary Battery Storage]
    Charger --> Meter[Smart Meter for Billing]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Define State of Charge (SOC) and Depth of Discharge (DOD) in a battery. How are they related?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **SOC** is the present capacity of a fully charged battery expressed as a percentage of its rated capacity. SOC = 100% means fully charged, SOC = 0% means fully discharged. (1.5 marks)
- **DOD** is the percentage of battery capacity that has been discharged relative to its rated capacity. DOD = 100% – SOC. (1 mark)
- They are complementary: **SOC + DOD = 100%**. (0.5 mark)

---

**Q2.** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Differentiate between Level 1 and Level 3 EV charging.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Level 1 (AC Slow):** Uses 120/230 V single-phase AC supply, 1.4–2.3 kW, charging time 8–18 h, no dedicated infrastructure. (1.5 marks)
- **Level 3 (DC Fast):** Uses 200–1000 V DC, 50–400 kW, charging time 15–40 min, bypasses onboard charger, requires dedicated high-power station. (1.5 marks)

---

### Part B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

---

**Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model]`

**(a)** Explain the **CC-CV charging algorithm** used for lithium-ion batteries with a neat sketch of the voltage and current profile. Why is the CV phase essential? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A **50 kWh** EV battery is to be charged from **SOC = 10%** to **SOC = 90%** using a **50 kW DC fast charger** with **88% efficiency**. Compute (i) energy drawn from the grid, (ii) charging time, and (iii) average C-rate if the pack is rated **400 V, 125 Ah**. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Q-A:**

**(a) CC-CV Charging Algorithm (7 marks):**
- The **CC-CV (Constant Current – Constant Voltage)** is the universal charging protocol for Li-ion cells. (1 mark)
- **Phase 1 (CC):** BMS regulates current to a set value (e.g., 0.5C–1C). Cell voltage rises linearly from ~3.0 V to upper cutoff **4.2 V/cell**. (2 marks)
- **Phase 2 (CV):** Voltage held constant at 4.2 V/cell; current exponentially decays as SOC approaches 100%. (1 mark)
- **Termination:** Charging ends when current falls below 0.05C (or 3–5% of rated). (1 mark)
- **Why CV is essential:** Prevents over-voltage, lithium plating, electrolyte oxidation, and thermal runaway. (1 mark)
- **Sketch description:** Plot of V(t) and I(t) vs time — V rises linearly then plateaus; I is flat then decays exponentially. (1 mark)

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Energy to be stored in battery**
$$
\Delta E_{stored} = (SOC_f - SOC_i) \times C_{pack} = (0.90 - 0.10) \times 50 = 40 \text{ kWh}
$$

**Step 2 — Energy drawn from grid (i)**
$$
E_{grid} = \dfrac{\Delta E_{stored}}{\eta} = \dfrac{40}{0.88} = 45.45 \text{ kWh}
$$

**Step 3 — Charging time (ii)**
$$
t = \dfrac{E_{grid}}{P_{chg}} = \dfrac{45.45}{50} = 0.909 \text{ h} = 54.5 \text{ min}
$$

**Step 4 — Average C-rate (iii)**
Charging current:
$$
I = \dfrac{P_{chg}}{V_{pack} \cdot \eta} = \dfrac{50{,}000}{400 \times 0.88} = 142 \text{ A}
$$

C-rate:
$$
C = \dfrac{I}{Q_{Ah}} = \dfrac{142}{125} = 1.14C
$$

**Answer:** (i) **45.45 kWh**, (ii) **54.5 min**, (iii) **1.14C** (rapid charging regime).

---

**Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]`

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **components and working of a DC fast-charging station** including rectifier, cooling, and communication. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare **conductive, inductive (wireless), and battery swapping** as EV energy-refueling methods. List **two real-world Indian/global examples** for each. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Q-B:**

**(a) DC Fast Charging Station (7 marks):**
- **Grid Interface:** 11 kV / 33 kV AC → step-down transformer → AC bus. (1 mark)
- **Active Rectifier (PFC + IGBT):** Converts AC to regulated DC (200–1000 V). Power factor correction keeps PF > 0.99. (2 marks)
- **DC-DC Converter:** Regulates output voltage to match EV pack voltage; provides galvanic isolation (typically via high-frequency transformer). (1 mark)
- **Cooling System:** Liquid cooling of cables, connectors, and power modules to handle 50–400 kW continuous load. (1 mark)
- **Controller & Communication:** OCPP 1.6/2.0.1 protocol between charger and CMS; **ISO 15118** for plug-and-charge and V2G. (1 mark)
- **Safety:** Insulation monitoring, emergency stop, ground fault detection. (1 mark)

**(b) Comparison (7 marks):**

| Method | Pros | Cons | Example |
|--------|------|------|---------|
| Conductive | High efficiency (92–95%), mature, fast | Cable wear, connector standardization | Tata Power EZ Charge, Tesla Supercharger |
| Inductive | No plug, weather-resistant, safe | Low efficiency (85–90%), slow, expensive | Qualcomm Halo (tested), WiTricity |
| Battery Swap | < 5 min turnaround, pack second-life | High CapEx, pack standardization needed | Nio Power Swap, Sun Mobility (India) |

Each method: 2 marks for explanation + 0.5 mark for each example × 2 examples = 1 mark.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Don't confuse AC and DC charging paths** — AC uses the onboard charger; DC bypasses it. Many students draw the wrong block diagram and lose 2–3 marks.
> - **C-rate units** are often written wrongly as "C" alone. Always state $\text{C} = I/Q_{Ah}$ explicitly.
> - In numericals, **include efficiency in the denominator** when computing grid-side energy. Forgetting this gives a charging time that is too short and loses 1 mark.
> - Do **not** compute charging time assuming 100% SOC delta from a DC fast charger — KTU papers usually stop at 80% SOC for DCFC. Read the SOC range carefully.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Battery charging** is the controlled energy transfer from grid to EV pack, mediated by the BMS using a **CC-CV** algorithm.
- Three charging levels: **Level 1 (AC slow), Level 2 (AC fast), Level 3 (DC fast)** — each with distinct power, time, and infrastructure.
- **DC fast charging** bypasses the onboard charger and feeds the pack directly at **50–400+ kW**; typically stops at 80% SOC to avoid the CV tail.
- **C-rate** = $I / Q_{cap}$ is the universal descriptor of charging/discharging speed; **1C = 1 hour** to full charge.
- Standard connectors: **CCS2 (India/EU), CHAdeMO (Japan), NACS (Tesla/US), GB/T (China)**.
- **Communication protocols:** **OCPP** (charger-to-CMS), **ISO 15118** (charger-to-EV, plug-and-charge, V2G).
- **Efficiency** is always < 100% — typically **85–95%** depending on level and ambient conditions.
- **Thermal management** is critical: heat generated $Q = I^2 R t$ scales with the square of current, so high-power DCFC requires active liquid cooling.
- **V2G (Vehicle-to-Grid)** turns the EV into a distributed energy resource — bidirectional power flow.
- **Battery swapping** is a non-charging alternative that achieves < 5 min "refueling" but requires standardization.
- Always carry **efficiency and SOC delta** in your numerical answers — these are the most-skipped steps in KTU valuation.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Gasoline Handling
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT527 - TRANSPORTATION SYSTEMS AND SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Gasoline Handling

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Gasoline Handling** refers to the complete envelope of engineering operations involved in the **receipt, storage, transfer, dispensing, and transport of gasoline** between refinery gate-points and the end-user (vehicle fuel tank), conducted under engineered controls that mitigate fire, explosion, toxic exposure, and environmental release hazards.

In the context of **Transportation Systems and Safety** (PESFT527), gasoline handling is treated as a **high-hazard hydrocarbon logistics discipline** governed by:

- **Flammability parameters** (Flash Point $\le -40^\circ\text{C}$, LEL/UEL, Auto-Ignition Temperature $\approx 280^\circ\text{C}$)
- **Vapour-pressure-driven loss control** (Reid Vapour Pressure, RVP)
- **Static-electricity dissipation protocols**
- **Vapour-recovery and spill-containment engineering**
- **ATEX/IECEx zoning** for classified electrical installations

The activity chain comprises: **Refinery → Bulk Terminal → Road/Rail Tanker → Service Station UST → Forecourt Dispenser → Vehicle Tank**.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — "The Angry Cat in a Glass Jar"**
> Imagine gasoline as an **angry, explosive cat** sealed inside a **glass jar** (the storage tank). The cat's *hiss* represents flammable vapours, its *scratches* represent static sparks, and its *heat* represents thermal energy. *Gasoline handling* is the science of keeping the jar **sealed, cool, grounded, and vented safely** so the cat can never escape, see a spark, or meet oxygen in the right proportion to ignite. Every rule — bonding, grounding, vapour recovery, vapour-balanced transfer — is just a different way of saying "do not let the cat out, and do not light a match near the jar."

## 1.3 Why Gasoline Demands Special Handling

| Property | Typical Value | Engineering Significance |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Flash Point** | $-43^\circ\text{C}$ (closed cup) | **Flammable liquid Class IA** (NFPA / UN 1203) |
| **Auto-Ignition Temperature** | $280^\circ\text{C}$ to $456^\circ\text{C}$ | Hot-surface ignition risk (exhaust manifolds) |
| **Vapour Density (Air = 1)** | **3.0 to 4.0** | Vapours **pool at low elevations** — explosion hazard in pits |
| **LEL / UEL** | **1.4 % / 7.6 %** | Narrow explosive envelope → small leaks become catastrophic |
| **Reid Vapour Pressure (RVP)** | **45 – 100 kPa** | High evaporative loss; mandates vapour recovery |
| **Dielectric Constant** | $\approx 2.0$ to $2.2$ | Accumulates **static charge** during flow |
| **Electrical Conductivity** | $\approx 1 \times 10^{-14} \text{ S/m}$ | Requires **additive doping** (Stadis 450) to dissipate charge |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Five Pillars of Safe Gasoline Handling

### Pillar 1 — Vapour Control
Gasoline continuously releases vapours that form **explosive mixtures with air** within the **LEL–UEL window (1.4 % – 7.6 %)**. Two engineered controls exist:

1. **Vapour-Balanced (Stage II) Recovery** — displaced vapours from the receiving tank are routed back to the delivering tank via a coaxial hose.
2. **Vapour-Recovery (Stage I)** — vapours displaced during tanker unloading are captured by a **vapour-recovery unit (VRU)** at the terminal.

### Pillar 2 — Static-Electricity Dissipation
Liquid flow through pipes, filters, and hoses generates **triboelectric charge**. The charge accumulation rate is governed by:

$$I = Q \cdot v \cdot A$$

where:
- $I$ = generated current (Amperes)
- $Q$ = space-charge density ($\text{C/m}^3$)
- $v$ = linear velocity ($\text{m/s}$)
- $A$ = cross-sectional area ($\text{m}^2$)

The industry rule of thumb is the **"Bonding, Grounding, Relaxation" triad** — flow velocity at the nozzle must be **$\le 7 \text{ m/s}$** (API RP 2003) and a **30-second relaxation time** must be observed before disconnecting.

### Pillar 3 — Fire & Explosion Prevention
The **Fire Triangle** must be broken: **Fuel + Oxygen + Ignition Source** must never coexist. Engineering controls include:
- **Bonding** (resistance $\le 1 \text{ M}\Omega$) and **grounding** (resistance $\le 10 \text{ }\Omega$)
- Explosion-relief venting sized using the **NFPA 68** equation
- **ATEX/IECEx** classified equipment in zones

### Pillar 4 — Spill Containment
Underground Storage Tanks (USTs) require **double-wall construction** with **interstitial leak monitoring**; aboveground tanks require **bunds/dikes** sized to **110 % of the largest tank volume**.

### Pillar 5 — Toxicological & Dermal Protection
**TLV-TWA = 300 ppm** (ACGIH). **Chronic exposure** to BTEX (Benzene, Toluene, Ethylbenzene, Xylenes) is carcinogenic — Benzene is a **Group 1 IARC carcinogen**. PPE includes nitrile gloves, vapour respirators, and splash goggles.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!NOTE]
> The following equations are **direct KTU high-yield items** — they frequently appear in numerical Part A and Part B questions.

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\textbf{(i) Vapour-Balanced Mass Conservation:} && m_{\text{delivered}} = m_{\text{received}} + m_{\text{vapour displaced}} \\
&\textbf{(ii) Reid Vapour Pressure Equivalent (DVPE):} && \text{DVPE} = \text{RVP} - 6.9 \text{ kPa} \\
&\textbf{(iii) Static-Charge Relaxation Time:} && \tau = \varepsilon \varepsilon_0 \rho \quad [\text{seconds}] \\
&\textbf{(iv) Maximum Safe Flow Velocity (API RP 2003):} && v_{\max} = 7 \text{ m/s} \\
&\textbf{(v) Tank Vapour Space (Tesco) Ratio:} && \eta = \dfrac{V_{\text{vapour}}}{V_{\text{tank}}} \le 0.20 \\
&\textbf{(vi) Bund Volume (110 % Rule):} && V_{\text{bund}} = 1.10 \times V_{\text{largest tank}} \\
&\textbf{(vii) Explosion Venting Area (NFPA 68):} && A_v = \dfrac{C \cdot V^{2/3}}{P_{\text{red}}^{0.5}} \\
&\textbf{(viii) Flammability Limit Mixture Rule:} && L_{\text{mix}} = \dfrac{100}{\sum \dfrac{y_i}{L_i}} \quad \text{(Le Chatelier)} \\
&\textbf{(ix) Evaporative Loss (RVP-based):} && E = 0.0241 \cdot \text{RVP}^{0.65} \quad [\text{litres/m}^3\text{/day}] \\
&\textbf{(x) Bonding Resistance Criterion:} && R_{\text{bond}} \le 1 \text{ M}\Omega
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Constants to Memorize:** $\varepsilon_0 = 8.854 \times 10^{-12} \text{ F/m}$; Gasoline $\rho \approx 740 \text{ kg/m}^3$; $1 \text{ gallon (US)} = 3.785 \text{ L}$.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application Domain | Engineering Use of Gasoline-Handling Theory |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Refinery Logistics** | Sizing of vapour-recovery units, flare header design |
| **Forecourt Retail** | Stage II vapour-recovery nozzle ergonomics & maintenance |
| **Tanker Truck Design** | Co-axial vapour-recovery hoses, rollover valves, bottom-loading |
| **Pipeline Transfer** | Anti-static additive dosing (Stadis 450), velocity control |
| **Regulatory Compliance** | OISD-179 (India), API RP 2003, NFPA 30, IS 1448 |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Maximum Safe Flow Velocity from Charge Density Limit

The **charge-generation current** in a flowing hydrocarbon must not exceed the rate at which it can leak to ground. The classical derivation follows.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Statement of the Problem:** Determine the maximum safe bulk flow velocity of gasoline in a transfer pipe such that the generated static current does not exceed the leakage-discharge current, thereby preventing **charge accumulation $> 1 \text{ }\mu\text{C/m}^3$** (charge density that can produce incendive sparks).

**Step 1 — Define space-charge density.**
Space charge per unit volume generated due to flow is empirically given by:

$$Q = k \cdot v^n$$

where $k \approx 1.5 \times 10^{-9} \text{ C·s/m}^4$ for gasoline without anti-static additive, and $n \approx 1.7$ to $2.0$ (turbulent regime).

**Step 2 — Express generated current per unit length.**

$$I = Q \cdot v \cdot A$$

Substituting $Q = k v^n$:

$$I = k v^{n+1} A$$

**Step 3 — Set the incendive charge-density limit.**
The threshold $Q_{\text{crit}} = 1 \times 10^{-6} \text{ C/m}^3$.

**Step 4 — Equate the two expressions and solve for $v$.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_{\text{crit}} &= k v^n \\
v_{\max} &= \left( \dfrac{Q_{\text{crit}}}{k} \right)^{1/n}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Numerical evaluation with $n = 2$.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
v_{\max} &= \left( \dfrac{1 \times 10^{-6}}{1.5 \times 10^{-9}} \right)^{1/2} \\
v_{\max} &= \left( 666.67 \right)^{1/2} \\
v_{\max} &\approx 25.8 \text{ m/s}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Engineering Conclusion:** The *theoretical* limit is $\sim 26 \text{ m/s}$, but **API RP 2003** applies a **safety factor of $\sim 4$** and the empirical observation of **$v_{\max} \approx 7 \text{ m/s}$** (often called the "**1 m/s rule per inch of nominal pipe diameter**") is adopted universally in KTU/industry problems.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Le Chatelier's Rule for Multi-Component Flammability Limit

**Problem:** A gasoline vapour stream contains three components with the following mole fractions and LELs: methane ($y_1 = 0.4$, $L_1 = 5\%$), propane ($y_2 = 0.4$, $L_2 = 2.1\%$), and $n$-butane ($y_3 = 0.2$, $L_3 = 1.8\%$). Determine the **mixture LEL**.

**Step 1 — Write Le Chatelier's equation.**

$$L_{\text{mix}} = \dfrac{100}{\sum \limits_{i=1}^{n} \dfrac{y_i}{L_i}}$$

where $y_i$ is the mole percent of component $i$ in the *fuel* mixture and $L_i$ is the LEL of the *pure* component in air.

**Step 2 — Compute each term in the denominator.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dfrac{y_1}{L_1} &= \dfrac{40}{5.0} = 8.000 \\
\dfrac{y_2}{L_2} &= \dfrac{40}{2.1} = 19.048 \\
\dfrac{y_3}{L_3} &= \dfrac{20}{1.8} = 11.111
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Sum the contributions.**

$$\sum \dfrac{y_i}{L_i} = 8.000 + 19.048 + 11.111 = 38.159$$

**Step 4 — Invert and scale.**

$$L_{\text{mix}} = \dfrac{100}{38.159} \approx 2.62 \text{ \% vol in air}$$

> **Interpretation:** A fuel mixture of this composition becomes explosive above **2.62 %** in air — far below methane's pure LEL of 5 %, illustrating that **gasoline blends are explosive at very low concentrations** (gasoline LEL $\approx 1.4\%$, even lower than this example).

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Bund Volume Compliance for a Tank Farm

**Problem:** A retail tank farm has three USTs of capacities $25{,}000$ L, $20{,}000$ L, and $15{,}000$ L. The largest tank contains gasoline ($\rho = 740 \text{ kg/m}^3$). Calculate the **minimum bund volume** if the bund wall has a 5 % freeboard allowance and the largest tank is 95 % full at the time of hypothetical rupture.

**Step 1 — Effective releasable volume from the largest tank.**

$$V_{\text{release}} = 0.95 \times 25{,}000 = 23{,}750 \text{ L} = 23.75 \text{ m}^3$$

**Step 2 — Apply 110 % containment rule.**

$$V_{\text{bund, gross}} = 1.10 \times V_{\text{release}} = 1.10 \times 23.75 = 26.125 \text{ m}^3$$

**Step 3 — Subtract freeboard volume (5 %).**

$$V_{\text{bund, net}} = V_{\text{bund, gross}} \times (1 - 0.05) = 26.125 \times 0.95 = 24.819 \text{ m}^3$$

**Step 4 — Convert to a square bund plan for execution.**
Assume a square bund with side $L$ and height $h = 0.6$ m:

$$L = \sqrt{\dfrac{V_{\text{bund, net}}}{h}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{24.819}{0.6}} \approx 6.43 \text{ m}$$

> **Conclusion:** A **6.43 m × 6.43 m × 0.6 m** bund satisfies both **110 % rule** and **5 % freeboard** for this tank-farm configuration.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Failure Mode Causation

| Failure Mode | Root Cause | Engineered Control |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Vapour Cloud Explosion | Vapour accumulation in low-lying area | Vapour-recovery, ATEX zoning, forced ventilation |
| Static Spark Ignition | Charge accumulation during transfer | Bonding, grounding, anti-static additive, $v \le 7$ m/s |
| Overfill Spill | Level gauge failure | Overfill prevention valve (OPV), auto-shutoff, Scully system |
| Bund Overflow | Rainwater accumulation in bund | Rainwater drainage with **double-valve isolation** |
| Hot-Work Ignition | Welding sparks near vents | **Hot-work permit system**, gas-testing, fire watch |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Architecture Flow — Tanker-to-UST Transfer Station

The following Mermaid diagram maps the engineered flow and safety interlock chain during a typical **bottom-loading transfer of gasoline from a road tanker to an Underground Storage Tank (UST)** at a retail outlet.

```mermaid

    B --> C[EarthContinuityCheck]

    D -- Yes --> F[OpenVapourRecoveryCoupling]

    H --> I[ScullyOverfillProbeEngaged]
    I --> J[InitiateStageIVapourBalancedFlow]
    J --> K{FlowVelocityWithin7ms}
    K -- No --> L[ThrottleToSafeVelocity]
    K -- Yes --> M[RealTimeATEXMonitoring]
    M --> N[TankFillReaches95Percent]

    O --> P[30SecondRelaxationWait]
    P --> Q[DisconnectHoseAndVapourCoupling]

    style A fill:#cfe2ff,stroke:#0d6efd

```

## 4.2 Functional Architecture — Static-Electricity Hazard Control Loop

```mermaid
    T2 -- EarthPath10Ohm --> T3[ReceivingUST]

    T5 -- Yes --> T6[ContinueFlow]
    T5 -- No --> T7[ThrottleToSafe]
    T6 -- AntiStaticAdditive --> T8[ConductivityBoost]
    T8 -- Relaxation30s --> T9[SafeDisconnect]
```

## 4.3 Conceptual Block Diagram — Gasoline-Handling Subsystems

```mermaid

    subgraph INTAKE["INTAKE  RECEIPT"]
        I1[TankerUnloading]
        I2[StageIVapourRecovery]
    end

    end
```
