```mermaid
    S1 -. Spill .-> F2
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mark-Deduction Traps**
> 1. Confusing **Flash Point** with **Fire Point** (Fire Point $\approx$ Flash Point $+5^\circ\text{C}$ to $+10^\circ\text{C}$).
> 2. Quoting **LEL** of gasoline as **1.4 %** but forgetting the **UEL is 7.6 %** — both are required in Part A.
> 3. Writing the bond-resistance criterion as "**$\le 1 \text{ }\Omega$**" — the correct value is **$\le 1 \text{ M}\Omega$** (note the mega prefix).
> 4. Forgetting that vapour density $\gt 1$ means vapours **settle low** (creep along floor) — major pitfall in confined-space questions.
> 5. Not mentioning **Stage I and Stage II** vapour-recovery distinction in Part B descriptive answers.

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. Define Flash Point. Why is gasoline classified as a Class IA flammable liquid?**
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Model Paper Cycle 2]**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- **Definition (1 Mark):** Flash point is the **lowest temperature** at which a liquid gives off enough vapour to form an **ignitable mixture with air** near its surface, identified by a flash when an ignition source is applied (closed-cup tester, IS 1448 / ASTM D93).
- **Gasoline Flash Point (1 Mark):** Gasoline has a flash point of $\mathbf{-43^\circ\text{C}}$, well below the **operational ambient range** ($15^\circ\text{C}$–$45^\circ\text{C}$); hence, at *all* working temperatures it liberates ignitable vapours.
- **Class IA Classification (1 Mark):** Per **NFPA 30 / OISD-179**, a liquid with flash point $\lt 22.8^\circ\text{C}$ and boiling point $\lt 37.8^\circ\text{C}$ is **Class IA** — the most hazardous flammable-liquid class, mandating explosion-proof equipment, bonding, grounding, and double-walled storage.

---

### **Q2. List any three engineered safety controls used during bulk gasoline transfer from a road tanker to a UST.**
**[KTU University Exam – June 2023]**

**Model Answer (3 Marks — 1 Mark Each):**
1. **Bonding and Grounding:** Bonding cable ($\le 1 \text{ M}\Omega$) and ground rod ($\le 10 \text{ }\Omega$) eliminate potential difference between tanker and UST.
2. **Scully Overfill Prevention Probe:** Optical/thermistor probe sends a **5-second warning** at 90 % fill and **auto-shutoff** at 95 % fill.
3. **Vapour-Balanced (Stage I) Recovery:** Coaxial hose returns displaced vapours to the tanker, eliminating atmospheric release and avoiding vapour-cloud formation.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> Per KTU 2024 Scheme, **Module Internal Choice** mandates that the student answers **either** Question A **or** Question B. Both options are presented below for practice.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *Vapour Control & Static Hazards*
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 4 Internal Choice, Set A]**

#### (a) Explain in detail the **principle of operation, components, and safety interlocks** of a Stage II Vapour-Recovery system used at fuel dispensers. Highlight the engineering significance of the bell-nozzle and coaxial hose. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**1. Operating Principle (2 Marks):**
Stage II vapour recovery is a **vapour-balanced closed-loop transfer** system. As gasoline flows from the underground tank through the dispenser hose into the vehicle tank, the **displaced fuel vapours** in the vehicle tank are drawn back through the same hose and returned to the UST, thereby **preventing atmospheric release** of hydrocarbon vapours. The system maintains vapour/liquid equilibrium through a coaxial hose and an in-station vapour-recovery pump.

**2. Key Components (3 Marks):**
- **Co-axial hose:** Outer annulus returns vapours; inner core delivers liquid.
- **Bell-nozzle (vapour-collection nozzle):** Forms a vapour-tight seal around the vehicle's filler neck; equipped with an **automatic shutoff latch** that terminates flow when the tank is full.
- **Vapour-recovery pump (typically vane-type):** Creates the slight vacuum needed to drive vapour return.
- **In-station electronics:** ATEX-certified flow-meter and vapour-flow sensor.
- **Pressure/Vacuum (P/V) vent valve on UST:** Allows 1–3 inch water-column breathing range.

**3. Safety Interlocks (2 Marks):**
- **Bypass valve** activates if vapour line is blocked (prevents over-pressurization).
- **Spill container** under the dispenser captures drips.
- **Shear valve** at dispenser base — severs and seals the line on impact.

> **Engineering Significance:** The bell-nozzle + coaxial arrangement achieves **$\ge 95 \%$ vapour-capture efficiency** (per USEPA Tier II standards), reducing **reactive hydrocarbon emissions** and forecourt **odour/fire load**.

---

#### (b) Derive the relationship between flow velocity, static-charge density, and the API RP 2003 safe-velocity criterion. A gasoline transfer line is 75 mm in diameter. Compute the maximum safe volumetric flow rate if the recommended velocity is 7 m/s. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**1. Derivation (4 Marks):**

Charge-generation current per unit length:

$$I = Q \cdot v \cdot A \quad \text{(Coulombs per second per metre)}$$

where $Q$ is space-charge density. The charge-relaxation time for gasoline is:

$$\tau = \varepsilon \varepsilon_0 \rho \quad [\text{s}]$$

Substituting $\varepsilon \approx 2.1$, $\varepsilon_0 = 8.854 \times 10^{-12}$ F/m, $\rho \approx 2 \times 10^{14} \text{ }\Omega\text{-m}$:

$$\tau \approx 2.1 \times 8.854 \times 10^{-12} \times 2 \times 10^{14} \approx 3.72 \text{ s}$$

To avoid charge accumulation, the **residence time in the system** must be $\ge 3\tau$ (i.e., $\sim 11$ s). The empirical API RP 2003 limit $v \le 7$ m/s ensures residence time in a typical 30-m hose is $\sim 4.3$ s, which combined with **anti-static additive Stadis 450** raises conductivity to $50 \text{ pS/m}$ and drops $\tau$ to $\lt 0.5$ s — making the criterion safe.

**2. Numerical Computation (3 Marks):**

Pipe area:

$$A = \dfrac{\pi}{4} (0.075)^2 = 4.418 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2$$

Maximum volumetric flow:

$$Q_v = A \cdot v_{\max} = 4.418 \times 10^{-3} \times 7 = 0.03093 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$

Converting to L/min:

$$Q_v = 0.03093 \times 1000 \times 60 = 1855.7 \text{ L/min} \approx 1856 \text{ L/min}$$

> **Final Answer:** The maximum safe flow rate is **$\approx 1.86$ m³/min (1856 L/min)** for a 75 mm line at 7 m/s.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Storage, Spill Containment & Bund Design*
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Module 4 Internal Choice, Set B]**

#### (a) Compare **Underground Storage Tanks (UST)** vs. **Above-Ground Storage Tanks (AST)** for gasoline, listing at least four engineering criteria. Explain why USTs dominate urban retail outlets. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

| Criterion | UST | AST |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Fire Exposure Risk** | Low (buried, isolated from ignition) | High (visible, exposed to ambient fires) |
| **Evaporative Loss (RVP)** | Low (cool subsurface temperature) | High (solar heating elevates RVP) |
| **Leak Detection** | **Mandatory** interstitial monitoring + SIR | Visual inspection; bund/dike alarms |
| **CAPEX** | Higher (excavation, double-wall, cathodic protection) | Lower (no excavation) |
| **Land Use** | Buried → minimal footprint | Requires **110 % bund area** |
| **Inspection Access** | Limited (ATG probes) | Full external visual |
| **Regulatory Burden** | OISD-179, CPCB, USEPA UST regs | OISD-118, factory inspectorate |
| **Urban Suitability** | **Preferred** (no visible tank, lower fire load) | Restricted (fire-NOC issues) |

**Why USTs dominate urban retail (2 Marks within 7):**
- Concealed from public view → **urban aesthetics**.
- Lower fire-radiative load on adjacent buildings.
- Reduced evaporative loss due to constant **~18°C subsurface** temperature.
- Compatible with **Stage II vapour recovery** at forecourt.

> **Examiner's Note:** Marks are awarded for **at least 4 distinct criteria** plus a 2-line justification of urban preference.

---

#### (b) A retail outlet has two USTs of $20{,}000$ L and $15{,}000$ L and one AST of $10{,}000$ L for emergency generator fuel. Calculate: **(i)** the minimum bund volume for the AST, **(ii)** the volumetric flow rate of recovered vapour during unloading of the $20{,}000$ L UST (assume $RVP = 65 \text{ kPa}$, temperature = $30^\circ\text{C}$), and **(iii)** the time required to fill a 50 L vehicle tank from a 25 mm dispenser hose at $7$ m/s. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**(i) Bund Volume (2 Marks):**
Largest AST = 10,000 L; 110 % rule:

$$V_{\text{bund}} = 1.10 \times 10{,}000 = 11{,}000 \text{ L} = 11.0 \text{ m}^3$$

**(ii) Vapour Recovery Flow Rate (3 Marks):**
Vapour volume displaced = liquid volume at loading (assuming no thermal contraction):

$$V_{\text{vapour}} = 20{,}000 \text{ L} = 20 \text{ m}^3$$

This vapour must be returned through the vapour line; volumetric flow at standard condition:

$$\dot{V}_{\text{vapour}} = \dfrac{V_{\text{vapour}}}{t_{\text{transfer}}}$$

Assuming a typical bottom-loading transfer time of $t = 30 \text{ min} = 1800 \text{ s}$:

$$\dot{V}_{\text{vapour}} = \dfrac{20}{1800} = 1.111 \times 10^{-2} \text{ m}^3/\text{s} = 11.1 \text{ L/s}$$

**(iii) Vehicle Fill Time (2 Marks):**

Hose area:

$$A = \dfrac{\pi}{4} (0.025)^2 = 4.909 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2$$

Volumetric flow:

$$Q_v = 4.909 \times 10^{-4} \times 7 = 3.436 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3/\text{s} = 3.436 \text{ L/s}$$

Fill time for 50 L:

$$t = \dfrac{50}{3.436} = 14.55 \text{ s} \approx 14.6 \text{ s}$$

> **Final Answers:** **(i) 11.0 m³**, **(ii) 11.1 L/s**, **(iii) 14.6 s**.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Gasoline Handling (Module 4)**

- ☐ **Class IA** flammable liquid (Flash Point $-43^\circ\text{C}$, BP $\lt 35^\circ\text{C}$).
- ☐ **LEL = 1.4 %, UEL = 7.6 %** — both must be quoted together.
- ☐ Vapour density $\approx$ 3 to 4 → **vapours pool at low elevation** (creep into drains, pits).
- ☐ **Auto-Ignition** $\approx$ 280°C — beware hot exhaust manifolds.
- ☐ **API RP 2003:** $v_{\max} = 7$ m/s; **API RP 2001:** $R_{\text{bond}} \le 1 \text{ M}\Omega$.
- ☐ **30-second relaxation** before disconnecting hoses.
- ☐ **Anti-static additive (Stadis 450)** raises conductivity to $\ge 50 \text{ pS/m}$.
- ☐ **Stage I** Vapour Recovery = Tanker → UST (terminal); **Stage II** = UST → Vehicle (forecourt).
- ☐ **Bunding:** 110 % of largest tank; rainwater locked-out by **double isolation valves**.
- ☐ **Scully Probe** = optical/thermal overfill prevention (warning @ 90 %, shutoff @ 95 %).
- ☐ **TLV-TWA = 300 ppm** (ACGIH); Benzene is a **Group 1 carcinogen**.
- ☐ **Le Chatelier's rule** for multi-component LEL; memorize formula.
- ☐ **Reid Vapour Pressure (RVP)** $\rightarrow$ **DVPE = RVP $-$ 6.9 kPa**.
- ☐ **NFPA 68** governs explosion-vent sizing: $A_v = C \cdot V^{2/3} \cdot P_{\text{red}}^{-0.5}$.
- ☐ **Evacuate downhill, upwind** in spill emergencies — vapours are heavier than air.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Other Safe Practices, Off the Road Motorized Equipment
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT527 - TRANSPORTATION SYSTEMS AND SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Other Safe Practices, Off the Road Motorized Equipment

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Off-the-Road Motorized Equipment (ORME)

### Formal Academic Definition
**Off-the-Road Motorized Equipment (ORME)** refers to a specialized class of self-propelled, motorized machinery designed primarily for operations on unpaved, uneven, or rugged terrains where conventional on-road vehicles cannot safely or efficiently function. These machines are engineered for specialized tasks in **construction, mining, agriculture, forestry, material handling, and military logistics**, and are explicitly excluded from registration under standard highway motor vehicle codes.

> [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** Off-the-Road Motorized Equipment includes any motorized machine used in work zones, construction sites, quarries, and agricultural fields that does not meet the Federal Motor Vehicle Safety Standards (FMVSS) for on-road public highway use, but requires rigorous safety protocols due to high risk of operator fatality.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
Think of ORME as the "heavy-duty workhorses" of industrial and infrastructure projects. While your regular car is like a *delicate commuter* built for paved roads and comfort, off-road equipment is like a *battle-ready utility machine* — bulky, powerful, and built for the roughest environments. Imagine a JCB excavator at a Kerala highway construction site near Munnar hills: it crawls over loose gravel, mud, and steep inclines where no sedan could ever go. The trade-off? Greater mechanical power comes with **greater kinetic energy, higher center of gravity, and reduced stability**, making safety management absolutely critical.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction:** On-road vehicles are governed by the Motor Vehicles Act (India, 1988), whereas off-road equipment is governed by **Factory Act, BOCW (Building & Other Construction Workers) Act, IS/ISO safety standards**, and OEM (Original Equipment Manufacturer) operational manuals.

### Classification of Off-the-Road Motorized Equipment

| Category | Typical Equipment | Primary Application |
|---|---|---|
| **Earthmoving Equipment** | Bulldozers, Excavators, Backhoe Loaders, Skid-Steer Loaders | Site preparation, trenching, grading |
| **Material Handling** | Forklifts, Telehandlers, Cranes, Conveyors | Loading, stacking, lifting |
| **Compaction Equipment** | Road Rollers, Vibratory Compactors, Plate Compactors | Soil and asphalt compaction |
| **Mining Equipment** | Dump Trucks (off-highway), Drilling Rigs, Loaders | Mineral extraction operations |
| **Agricultural Equipment** | Tractors, Combine Harvesters, Power Tillers | Farming operations |
| **Specialty Vehicles** | ATVs (All-Terrain Vehicles), Side-by-Sides (SxS) | Inspection, survey, light transport |

## 1.2 Other Safe Practices in Transportation Systems

### Formal Definition
**Other Safe Practices** encompass supplementary, non-vehicular safety protocols that complement primary accident-prevention measures. These practices address **human behavioral, environmental, organizational, and procedural** dimensions of safety beyond equipment and infrastructure controls.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition:** Other Safe Practices refer to the holistic set of safety behaviors and organizational protocols — including emergency preparedness, incident reporting, ergonomics, fatigue management, work-zone traffic control, and environmental hazard mitigation — that collectively form the safety management system (SMS) of a transportation project.

### Intuitive Analogy
If primary safety measures (seatbelts, helmets, signaling) are like the **"bones"** of a safety system, then "Other Safe Practices" are the **"muscles and nerves"** that make the system actually function. A helmet on a hook is useless until someone consciously puts it on — that conscious choice is what "other safe practices" cultivate. Think of it as the difference between *having fire extinguishers* (primary) and *knowing how to use them, conducting mock drills, and reporting hazards* (supplementary practices).

> [!NOTE]
> **Haddon Matrix Perspective:** Other Safe Practices primarily address the **pre-event phase** (prevention) and **post-event phase** (mitigation, response) of the Haddon Matrix — the epidemiological framework used globally in transportation safety engineering.

---

## 1.3 Why These Topics Matter in KTU Examinations

KTU 2024 Scheme examiners frequently test:
- **Identification** of ORME categories and their hazards (3-mark direct questions)
- **Application** of safety controls (ROPS, FOPS, seat belts, backup alarms) to specific scenarios (7-mark analytical)
- **Evaluation** of safe work practices and procedural compliance (14-mark comprehensive)

> [!TIP] > **Examination Tip:** Always link every safety recommendation to either **Engineering Control, Administrative Control, or PPE** — the universally accepted **Hierarchy of Controls** in occupational safety.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Foundation: Hazard Categories of ORME

The theoretical basis of ORME safety rests on the **energy release model** of accidents (per William Haddon's injury epidemiology). ORME concentrates enormous **kinetic energy**, **potential energy** (elevated loads), and **mechanical energy** (rotating shafts, hydraulics) into compact operating envelopes, creating three primary hazard classes:

### 2.1.1 The Three Primary Hazard Classes

**Hazard Class A — Machine-Related Hazards (Intrinsic)**
- **Rollover** (longitudinal/lateral): Caused by elevated center of gravity on slopes
- **Tipping** during load handling
- **Falling objects** from buckets or forks
- **Crushing** by hydraulic arms or undercarriage

**Hazard Class B — Environmental Hazards (Contextual)**
- Uneven terrain, soft soil, mud
- Overhead power lines (electrocution)
- Underground utilities (gas, water, fiber)
- Confined space entry

**Hazard Class C — Human/Behavioral Hazards (Operational)**
- Operator fatigue
- Lack of PPE compliance
- Inadequate training
- Substance impairment
- Failure to perform pre-operational checks

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Statistical Reality:** According to global occupational safety data, **rollovers account for approximately 50% of ORME operator fatalities**, and **struck-by/caught-in accidents account for another 30%**. Both are largely preventable through engineering controls (ROPS, seat belts) and administrative controls (training, slope limits).

## 2.2 Core Safety Controls for ORME

### 2.2.1 ROPS — Roll-Over Protective Structure

**Definition:** A cab or frame structure designed to maintain a survival zone for the operator when the machine experiences a complete 180° inversion.

**Engineering Principle:** The ROPS must withstand, without exceeding allowable deflection, an energy impulse calculated as:

$$
E_{ROPS} = m_{machine} \cdot g \cdot h_{cg}
$$

Where:
- $m_{machine}$ = machine mass in **kg**
- $g$ = gravitational acceleration (9.81 m/s²)
- $h_{cg}$ = height of center of gravity in **m**
- $E_{ROPS}$ = potential energy at rollover in **Joules**

### 2.2.2 FOPS — Falling Object Protective Structure

**Definition:** A reinforced roof structure designed to absorb impact energy from falling debris, rated under **ISO 3449** (Level I, II, or III depending on impact energy threshold).

### 2.2.3 Other Engineering Controls

| Control | Function | Standard Reference |
|---|---|---|
| **Seat Belt** | Restrains operator within ROPS survival zone | ISO 6683 |
| **Backup Alarm** | Audible warning during reverse operation | SAE J994 |
| **Fire Extinguisher** | First-response fire suppression | IS 2190 |
| **Falling Object Guard** | Shields operator from debris | ISO 3449 |
| **Side Guards** | Prevents entanglement in rotating parts | ISO 3457 |
| **Visibility Aids** | Cameras, mirrors, proximity sensors | OEM-specific |

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula & Concept Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table is the **high-yield reference** for KTU 2024 ESE questions. While ORME safety is largely qualitative, a few quantitative concepts (slope stability, stopping distance, center of gravity) are examinable.

| Concept | Formula / Definition | Application Context | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| **ROPS Energy Threshold** | $E = m \cdot g \cdot h_{cg}$ | Rollover energy absorption design | Joules (J) |
| **Maximum Safe Slope** | $\tan(\theta) \leq \frac{w/2}{h_{cg}}$ | Static stability on inclined surface | Degrees |
| **Stopping Distance** | $d = v \cdot t_r + \frac{v^2}{2 \mu g}$ | Braking distance on loose surface | m |
| **Swing Radius Hazard** | $r_{tail} = L_{rear} \cdot \omega$ | Excavator counterweight strike zone | m |
| **Load Capacity Reduction** | $W_{actual} = W_{rated} \times (1 - 0.01\Delta h)$ | Forklift load on uneven terrain | kg |
| **Visibility Cone** | $\theta_{blind} = 360° - \sum \theta_{visible}$ | Operator blind-spot assessment | Degrees |
| **Noise Exposure Limit** | $T_{safe} = \frac{8}{2^{(L-85)/3}}$ | OSHA-style daily exposure hours | hours |

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical Note on Table Syntax:** In the formulas above, all vertical bars/divisions are rendered using `\cdot` or `\times` or `/` to avoid breaking markdown table parsing. Do NOT use `|` symbols inside the table.

## 2.4 "Other Safe Practices" — Theoretical Framework

The **Swiss Cheese Model** (Reason, 1990) explains why accidents occur despite multiple safeguards. Each layer (slice of cheese) has holes (failures), and when all holes align, an accident results. "Other Safe Practices" aim to **distribute holes non-alignently** across multiple layers.

### Key Procedural Frameworks

**1. Pre-Operational Safety Protocol**
- Daily inspection checklist (5-min walk-around)
- Fluid level verification (oil, coolant, hydraulic, fuel)
- Tire/track condition
- Control function verification
- Safety device function test (ROPS, alarms, lights)

**2. Operational Safety Protocol**
- Site-specific hazard assessment
- Communication protocol (hand signals, radio)
- Speed limit adherence
- Spotter deployment in blind zones
- Shift handover documentation

**3. Post-Operational Safety Protocol**
- Parking on level ground
- Lowering implements to ground
- Engine shut-down, key removal
- Lock-out/Tag-out (LOTO) for maintenance
- Daily log submission

> [!TIP]
> **Remember the "3 P's" of Other Safe Practices:** **P**revention (planning), **P**rotection (controls), and **P**reparedness (emergency response). This is a favorite KTU examiner phrase for full-mark answers.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Sector | Application |
|---|---|
| **Highway Construction** | Compaction rollers, pavers, excavators for NH/SH projects |
| **Urban Metro Rail** | Tunnel boring equipment, cranes, mobile lighting towers |
| **Airport Runways** | Snowplows, friction testers, heavy sweepers |
| **Defence/Strategic** | Bulldozers, terrain loaders in border infrastructure |
| **Kerala PWD Projects** | Landslide clearance, hill road construction in Western Ghats |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Maximum Safe Slope for Static Stability of an ORME

This derivation appears in KTU 14-mark questions and is a critical "engineering" component of the otherwise qualitative subject.

### Problem Setup
Consider a wheel-loader or forklift of mass $m$, wheelbase $w$, and center of gravity at height $h_{cg}$ above the ground. Determine the maximum slope angle $\theta_{max}$ at which the machine can be statically parked without tipping about its downhill axle.

### Step 1: Free-Body Diagram
The machine on an inclined surface (angle $\theta$) experiences:
- Weight $W = m \cdot g$ acting vertically downward through the center of gravity (CG)
- Normal reaction $N$ acting perpendicular to the slope at the downhill wheel
- Friction force $F$ acting along the slope at the wheels

The weight vector can be resolved into two components relative to the slope:
- Component parallel to slope (causing sliding): $W_{\parallel} = W \sin\theta$
- Component perpendicular to slope (causing tipping moment): $W_{\perp} = W \cos\theta$

### Step 2: Tipping Condition
Tipping occurs when the line of action of the resultant weight vector passes through the **downhill wheel contact point**, creating zero moment about that point. At this critical condition, the CG lies directly above the contact patch.

### Step 3: Geometric Relationship
From the geometry of the right triangle formed by the CG, the wheel contact point, and the wheelbase axis:

$$
\tan(\theta_{max}) = \frac{\text{Horizontal distance from CG to downhill wheel}}{h_{cg}}
$$

For a symmetric machine with CG centered laterally:

$$
\tan(\theta_{max}) = \frac{w/2}{h_{cg}}
$$

### Step 4: Final Expression

$$
\boxed{\theta_{max} = \arctan\left(\frac{w}{2 \cdot h_{cg}}\right)}
$$

### Step 5: Worked Numerical Example
A skid-steer loader has:
- Wheelbase $w = 1.2$ m
- Center of gravity height $h_{cg} = 0.9$ m (with operator)

Compute the maximum safe slope.

$$
\theta_{max} = \arctan\left(\frac{1.2}{2 \times 0.9}\right)
$$

$$
\theta_{max} = \arctan\left(\frac{1.2}{1.8}\right)
$$

$$
\theta_{max} = \arctan(0.667)
$$

$$
\theta_{max} \approx 33.7^{\circ}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Safety Application:** Manufacturers typically recommend operating at **no more than 50–60% of this theoretical maximum** to provide a safety margin for dynamic loads, braking forces, and uneven terrain. So practical operating limit ≈ 17–20° for this loader.

## 3.2 Derivation: Rollover Energy for ROPS Design

### Step 1: Identify Energy Sources
During a 180° rollover, the machine's CG rises by approximately $2 \cdot h_{cg}$ (it ends up at the same height but on the other side, requiring the CG to traverse the full vertical diameter of the rotation).

### Step 2: Energy Released
The potential energy released in the rollover:

$$
E_{release} = m \cdot g \cdot \Delta h = m \cdot g \cdot 2h_{cg}
$$

### Step 3: Energy That Must Be Absorbed
The ROPS must absorb this energy through controlled deformation without allowing intrusion into the operator survival zone. The required energy absorption capacity:

$$
E_{ROPS,min} = m \cdot g \cdot 2 h_{cg}
$$

### Step 4: Worked Example
A 15,000 kg bulldozer has $h_{cg} = 1.5$ m (with operator cab loaded).

$$
E_{ROPS,min} = 15{,}000 \times 9.81 \times 2 \times 1.5
$$

$$
E_{ROPS,min} = 15000 \times 9.81 \times 3.0
$$

$$
E_{ROPS,min} = 441{,}450 \text{ J} \approx 441 \text{ kJ}
$$

This is the energy that the ROPS structure must dissipate through deformation. ISO 3471 specifies testing protocols where this energy is delivered as an impact impulse.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Hazard–Control Mapping for ORME

This matrix is **examination gold** — examiners love asking students to map hazards to controls.

| Hazard | Engineering Control | Administrative Control | PPE |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Rollover** | ROPS, slope sensors, tilt alarms | Slope limit policy, no-go zones, training | Seat belt (3-point) |
| **Struck-by (falling object)** | FOPS, load backrest (forklift) | Exclusion zone, spotters, lift plan | Hard hat, steel-toe boots |
| **Struck-by (machine swing)** | Rear-view camera, proximity sensors | Spotter, barricades, signage | Hi-vis vest |
| **Caught-in/between** | Guarding, lock-out/tag-out | Shutdown protocol, energy isolation | No loose clothing, gloves |
| **Noise (>85 dBA)** | Sound-dampening cab, mufflers | Rotation of operators, exposure logs | Earplugs, earmuffs |
| **Vibration (HAVS)** | Anti-vibration mounts, suspension seats | Work-rest cycles | Anti-vibration gloves |
| **Dust (silica, coal)** | Enclosed cab, air filtration | Water suppression, exposure monitoring | P2/N95 respirator |
| **Heat stress** | Climate-controlled cab | Hydration policy, shift rotation | Cooling vests |
| **Electrocution (overhead lines)** | Insulated booms, proximity sensors | Safe clearance distance (per IS 5216) | Class E insulated gloves |
| **Fire/Explosion** | Fire suppression system, spark arrestors | Hot work permits, fuel handling SOP | Fire-resistant clothing |

## 3.4 Decision Tree: Pre-Operational Inspection (Logical Flow)

Use this analytical flow when answering "describe the pre-operational safety check" questions:

**Step 1:** Identity & Documentation — Verify operator certification, machine logbook, and valid fitness certificate.

**Step 2:** Walk-Around (5 minutes, 360°) — Check tires/tracks, leaks, structural cracks, lights, mirrors, glass.

**Step 3:** Fluid Levels — Engine oil, hydraulic fluid, coolant, fuel, brake fluid (per dipstick markings).

**Step 4:** Safety Devices Test — Horn, reverse alarm, beacons, ROPS/FOPS integrity, fire extinguisher charge, seat belt function.

**Step 5:** Control Function Test — Steering, brakes, hydraulics, transmission, gauges, emergency stop.

**Step 6:** Environmental Check — Ground stability, overhead lines, underground services (CAT & Genny scan), ventilation in confined spaces.

**Step 7:** Sign-Off — Operator signs daily inspection logbook; supervisor co-signs critical equipment.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Expected Answer:** Students should mention the **"Five Senses Check"** — Look, Listen, Smell, Touch, Test — as a memorable framework for pre-operational inspections.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: ORME Safety Management System

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the hierarchical safety management structure for off-road equipment operations, mapping the interactions between management, equipment, personnel, and environment.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[SMS Policy & Commitment] --> B[Hazard Identification & Risk Assessment]
    B --> C[Engineering Controls Layer]
    B --> D[Administrative Controls Layer]
    B --> E[PPE Controls Layer]
    
    C --> C1[ROPS and FOPS Installation]
    C --> C2[Backup Alarms and Cameras]
    C --> C3[Load Moment Indicators]
    C --> C4[Safety Interlocks and Limit Switches]
    
    D --> D1[Operator Certification Program]
    D --> D2[Daily Inspection Protocols]
    D --> D3[Permit to Work System]
    D --> D4[Site Speed Limits and No Go Zones]
    
    E --> E1[Hard Hats and Safety Footwear]
    E --> E2[Hearing Protection]
    E --> E3[Hi Visibility Clothing]
    E --> E4[Respiratory Protection]
    
    F[Equipment Inventory and Maintenance] --> C
    G[Workforce Training and Competency] --> D
    H[PPE Supply and Maintenance] --> E
    
    I[Emergency Preparedness] --> J[Incident Response Plan]
    I --> K[First Aid Stations]
    I --> L[Fire Fighting Equipment]
    
    M[Audit and Review Loop] --> A
    C1 --> M
    D1 --> M
    E1 --> M
```

## 4.2 Process Flow: Operational Safety Sequence

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[Shift Start Briefing] --> S2[Pre Operational Inspection]
    S2 --> S3{Safety Check Pass}
    S3 -->|No| S4[Defect Reporting and Lockout]
    S4 --> S3
    S3 -->|Yes| S5[Site Hazard Assessment]
    S5 --> S6[Establish Exclusion Zone]
    S6 --> S7[Begin Operation with Spotter]
    S7 --> S8{Mid Shift Check}
    S8 -->|Issue| S9[Stop Work Authority]
    S8 -->|Normal| S10[Continue Operation]
    S9 --> S3
    S10 --> S11[End of Shift Shutdown]
    S11 --> S12[Post Operation Inspection]
    S12 --> S13[Document and Hand Over]
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram: Hierarchy of Controls for ORME Safety

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    L1[Most Effective] --> L2[Elimination]
    L2 --> L3[Substitution]
    L3 --> L4[Engineering Controls]
    L4 --> L5[Administrative Controls]
    L5 --> L6[PPE]
    L6 --> L7[Least Effective]
    
    L2 -. Example .-> E1[Remove hazard entirely]
    L3 -. Example .-> E2[Use safer alternative equipment]
    L4 -. Example .-> E3[ROPS, FOPS, Alarms, Guards]
    L5 -. Example .-> E4[Training, Procedures, Signs]
    L6 -. Example .-> E5[Helmets, Gloves, Boots]
```

## 4.4 Stakeholder Responsibility Matrix (RACI)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    R[Responsible Operator] --> A1[Executes Pre Operational Check]
    R --> A2[Reports Defects]
    R --> A3[Follows Safe Operating Procedure]
    
    A[Accountable Supervisor] --> A4[Verifies Certification]
    A --> A5[Conducts Site Risk Assessment]
    A --> A6[Approves Permit to Work]
    
    C[Consulted Safety Officer] --> A7[Conducts Safety Audit]
    C --> A8[Investigates Incidents]
    C --> A9[Updates SOPs]
    
    I[Informed Site Manager] --> A10[Receives Daily Reports]
    I --> A11[Allocates Resources]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation:** The RACI (Responsible, Accountable, Consulted, Informed) model clarifies that while the operator executes tasks, accountability for safety rests with the supervisor/manager, and expert input flows from the safety officer. This appears in KTU 14-mark questions on "organizational safety structure."

## 4.5 Schematic: Hazard Communication Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    H1[Hazard Detected] --> H2{Severity Assessment}
    H2 -->|Low| H3[Log in Daily Register]
    H2 -->|Medium| H4[Verbal Report to Supervisor]
    H2 -->|High| H5[Stop Work and Report]
    H4 --> H6[Supervisor Review]
    H5 --> H6
    H3 --> H7[Trend Analysis Monthly]
    H6 --> H7
    H7 --> H8[Corrective Action]
    H8 --> H9[Verification and Closeout]
    H9 --> H10[Feedback to Workforce]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**Define Off-the-Road Motorized Equipment (ORME). List any four categories with one example each.**

**Model Answer:**
Off-the-Road Motorized Equipment (ORME) refers to self-propelled, motorized machinery designed for operation on unpaved or rugged terrain, excluded from on-road motor vehicle registration. Four categories:

1. **Earthmoving Equipment** — Excavator (e.g., JCB 3DX)
2. **Material Handling Equipment** — Forklift (e.g., 3-ton diesel forklift)
3. **Compaction Equipment** — Vibratory road roller
4. **Agricultural Equipment** — Tractor with tiller attachment

[3 marks: 1 for definition + 2 for categories with examples]

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**What is the Hierarchy of Controls? List its levels in order of effectiveness.**

**Model Answer:**
The Hierarchy of Controls is a systematic framework used in occupational safety to determine the most effective intervention to reduce hazard exposure. Levels from most to least effective:

1. **Elimination** — Physically remove the hazard
2. **Substitution** — Replace with less hazardous option
3. **Engineering Controls** — ROPS, guards, alarms (isolate people from hazard)
4. **Administrative Controls** — Training, procedures, signage (change work behavior)
5. **Personal Protective Equipment (PPE)** — Helmets, gloves, ear protection (protect the worker)

[3 marks: 1 for definition + 2 for listing levels with brief description]

---

## Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Internal Choice — Question A OR Question B

---

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]

**(a)** Describe the major hazards associated with the operation of Off-the-Road Motorized Equipment (ORME) at a construction site. Discuss the engineering and administrative controls to mitigate these hazards. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** Explain the concept of ROPS and FOPS. A bulldozer of mass 18,000 kg has its center of gravity at 1.4 m height. Calculate the minimum energy absorption capacity required for its ROPS structure. **[7 Marks]**

#### Model Solution for Part (a):

**Major Hazards at Construction Sites:**

1. **Rollover/Tipping** — Uneven terrain, slope operation, sudden turns at high speed
2. **Struck-by Accidents** — Falling loads from buckets, swinging counterweights, moving machinery
3. **Caught-in/Between** — Rotating shafts, hydraulic components, crushing under tracks
4. **Collision** — With workers, other equipment, structures
5. **Electrocution** — Contact with overhead power lines (very common in urban India)
6. **Noise and Vibration** — Long-term hearing loss, hand-arm vibration syndrome
7. **Dust Exposure** — Silica dust leading to silicosis

**Engineering Controls:**

- **ROPS and FOPS** installation on all heavy equipment
- **Backup alarms and proximity sensors** to alert nearby workers
- **Load Moment Indicators (LMI)** on cranes to prevent overloading
- **Side guards and belly guards** to prevent entanglement
- **Enclosed, climate-controlled cabs** with air filtration
- **Visibility aids** — 360° cameras, mirrors, LED work lights
- **Automatic shutdown systems** for low oil/overheating

**Administrative Controls:**

- **Mandatory operator certification** (e.g., from the National Skill Development Corporation)
- **Daily pre-operational inspection checklists**
- **Permit-to-Work system** for high-risk activities
- **Designated traffic management plan** with speed limits and exclusion zones
- **Spotters/signallers** for blind-spot operations
- **Toolbox talks** and daily safety briefings
- **Drug and alcohol testing** policy
- **Fatigue management** with maximum shift hour limits

[7 marks: 2 for hazards, 2 for engineering controls, 2 for administrative controls, 1 for conclusion]

#### Model Solution for Part (b):

**Concept Explanation:**

- **ROPS (Roll-Over Protective Structure):** A cab or frame structure designed to protect the operator from injury if the machine rolls over. It maintains a survival zone (Safety Space Envelope) around the operator. ROPS is tested under **ISO 3471** standards.

- **FOPS (Falling Object Protective Structure):** A reinforced roof structure designed to protect the operator from falling objects (rocks, debris, load drops). It is rated under **ISO 3449** with three levels: Level I (5.4 kJ), Level II (11.6 kJ), Level III (17.8 kJ) impact energy.

**Calculation:**

Given:
- Mass of bulldozer, $m = 18{,}000$ kg
- Height of center of gravity, $h_{cg} = 1.4$ m
- Gravitational acceleration, $g = 9.81$ m/s²

During a 180° rollover, the CG rises by $\Delta h = 2 \times h_{cg} = 2 \times 1.4 = 2.8$ m.

The minimum energy the ROPS must absorb equals the released potential energy:

$$
E_{ROPS,min} = m \cdot g \cdot \Delta h
$$

$$
E_{ROPS,min} = 18{,}000 \times 9.81 \times 2.8
$$

$$
E_{ROPS,min} = 494{,}424 \text{ J}
$$

$$
\boxed{E_{ROPS,min} \approx 494.4 \text{ kJ}}
$$

[7 marks: 1 for ROPS concept, 1 for FOPS concept, 1 for ISO standards mention, 2 for formula application, 1 for substitution, 1 for final answer with units]

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**(a)** What are "Other Safe Practices" in transportation safety? Explain the Pre-Operational, Operational, and Post-Operational safety protocols for ORME. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** Describe the Haddon Matrix and Swiss Cheese Model as frameworks for transportation safety. Apply the Swiss Cheese Model to analyze a hypothetical ORME rollover accident. **[7 Marks]**

#### Model Solution for Part (a):

**Definition of Other Safe Practices:**
Other Safe Practices refer to the holistic set of organizational, behavioral, and procedural safety measures that complement primary engineering and equipment-based safety controls. They form the soft infrastructure of safety and address the human, environmental, and systemic dimensions of accident prevention.

**Pre-Operational Safety Protocol:**

- Verify operator certification and fitness (no alcohol, adequate rest)
- Conduct 5-minute walk-around inspection
- Check fluid levels (oil, hydraulic, coolant, fuel)
- Test safety devices: horn, reverse alarm, lights, ROPS, fire extinguisher
- Inspect tires/tracks, structural integrity
- Confirm site-specific risk assessment completion
- Ensure PPE availability for operator

**Operational Safety Protocol:**

- Establish exclusion zones around operating radius
- Deploy spotters/signallers for blind operations
- Maintain radio communication with site control
- Adhere to speed limits and slope restrictions
- Implement 3-point contact rule when mounting/dismounting
- No passengers other than the operator (unless designed otherwise)
- Stop work if any unsafe condition is observed
- Maintain 360° awareness, especially in reverse

**Post-Operational Safety Protocol:**

- Park on level ground, lower all implements to ground
- Apply parking brake, shut down engine, remove key
- Lock-out/Tag-out (LOTO) before any maintenance
- Clean equipment and remove debris
- Conduct end-of-shift inspection
- Complete operator logbook and hand over to next shift
- Report any defects or near-misses

[7 marks: 1 for definition, 2 each for three protocols]

#### Model Solution for Part (b):

**Haddon Matrix:**
The Haddon Matrix (William Haddon, 1968) is an epidemiological framework that applies the public health concepts of host, agent, and environment across three temporal phases: pre-event, event, and post-event. For transportation safety:

| | **Host (Human)** | **Agent (Vehicle/Equipment)** | **Environment (Road/Site)** |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Pre-Event** | Training, licensing, fitness | Brakes, tires, lights, ROPS | Signage, barriers, lighting |
| **Event** | Seat belt, helmet | Crumple zones, FOPS | Run-off area, crash cushions |
| **Post-Event** | First aid, EMS response | Fuel cut-off, fire suppression | Rescue access, traffic diversion |

**Swiss Cheese Model (James Reason, 1990):**
The Swiss Cheese Model conceptualizes safety as multiple defensive layers (slices of cheese), each with potential weaknesses (holes). An accident occurs when the holes in all layers align momentarily, allowing the hazard to reach the worker.

**Application to Hypothetical ORME Rollover:**

Consider a bulldozer rollover accident on a hill road construction site.

- **Layer 1 (Engineering Control):** ROPS was installed ✓, but the seat belt had a torn strap ✗ (hole)
- **Layer 2 (Administrative Control):** Site policy prohibits operation beyond 15° slope, but operator exceeded 22° ✗ (hole)
- **Layer 3 (Supervision):** Supervisor was off-site at the time ✗ (hole)
- **Layer 4 (Operator Behavior):** Operator was fatigued (worked 12 hours straight) ✗ (hole)
- **Layer 5 (Training):** Operator had basic training but not advanced slope-work certification ✗ (hole)
- **Layer 6 (PPE):** Hard hat was worn ✓, but high-visibility vest was missing ✗ (hole)

**Result:** All six holes aligned → bulldozer rolled on 22° slope → operator ejected (no seat belt) → crushed under ROPS structure → fatality.

**Lessons:** Multiple independent failures aligned. Even one functional layer (intact seat belt, active supervision, or training compliance) could have prevented fatality.

[7 marks: 1 each for Haddon Matrix and Swiss Cheese definitions, 2 for the matrix construction, 3 for the application example]

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in KTU Examinations:**
> 1. **Confusing ORME with on-road vehicles** — Examiners deduct marks if students cite Motor Vehicles Act provisions for ORME; the correct reference is the **Factory Act 1948, BOCW Act 1996, and IS/ISO standards**.
> 2. **Forgetting units in numerical answers** — Always append **J, m, kg, m/s²** to numerical values; a correct number without unit gets **zero credit** in engineering exams.
> 3. **Listing hazards without controls** — Naming "rollover" as a hazard is worth only 1 mark; describing the corresponding control (ROPS, slope limit, training) is what earns full marks.
> 4. **Skipping the Hierarchy of Controls** — For "suggest safety measures" questions, examiners expect students to **organize** the answer by Hierarchy of Controls (Elimination → PPE), not present a random list.
> 5. **Confusing ROPS with FOPS** — ROPS protects against **rollover**; FOPS protects against **falling objects**. Swapping these definitions is an instant 2-mark deduction.
> 6. **Not citing standards** — Always mention relevant **ISO/IS codes** (ISO 3471 for ROPS, ISO 3449 for FOPS, SAE J994 for alarms). This is the KTU examiner's "extra mile" marker for top grades.

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 ESE**

### ✅ Off-the-Road Motorized Equipment (ORME)

- [x] **ORME** = self-propelled machinery for unpaved/rugged terrain; excluded from highway registration
- [x] **Categories:** Earthmoving, Material Handling, Compaction, Mining, Agricultural, Specialty (ATVs/SxS)
- [x] **Top 3 Hazards:** Rollover (50%), Struck-by (30%), Caught-in/between (15%)
- [x] **ROPS** = Roll-Over Protective Structure (ISO 3471) — design energy $E = mg \cdot 2h_{cg}$
- [x] **FOPS** = Falling Object Protective Structure (ISO 3449) — Levels I/II/III
- [x] **Three Engineering Controls:** ROPS + Seat Belt + Backup Alarm (the "Big Three" of ORME safety)
- [x] **Maximum safe slope formula:** $\theta_{max} = \arctan\left(\frac{w}{2h_{cg}}\right)$ — always use 50–60% of theoretical value in practice

### ✅ Other Safe Practices

- [x] **Swiss Cheese Model (Reason, 1990)** — Multiple defense layers with holes; accident occurs when holes align
- [x] **Haddon Matrix** — Host × Agent × Environment × Pre/Event/Post phases
- [x] **Hierarchy of Controls:** Elimination → Substitution → Engineering → Administrative → PPE
- [x] **3 P's of Other Safe Practices:** Prevention, Protection, Preparedness
- [x] **Pre-Operational Protocol:** Identity → Walk-Around → Fluids → Safety Devices → Controls → Environment → Sign-Off
- [x] **Operational Protocol:** Exclusion Zone → Spotter → Communication → Speed Limit → Stop Work Authority
- [x] **Post-Operational Protocol:** Park → Lower → Shutdown → LOTO → Document → Handover
- [x] **RACI Model:** Responsible (operator), Accountable (supervisor), Consulted (safety officer), Informed (manager)

### ✅ Key Standards to Remember

- [x] **ISO 3471** — ROPS testing
- [x] **ISO 3449** — FOPS testing
- [x] **ISO 6683** — Seat belt requirement
- [x] **SAE J994** — Backup alarm standard
- [x] **IS 2190** — Fire extinguisher specification (India)
- [x] **BOCW Act 1996** — Building & Other Construction Workers Act (India)
- [x] **Factory Act 1948** — Governs ORME in industrial premises (India)

### ✅ Frequently Tested Numerical Concepts

- [x] Rollover energy: $E = m \cdot g \cdot 2h_{cg}$ — always use $2h_{cg}$ for 180° rollover
- [x] Safe slope: $\theta_{max} = \arctan\left(\frac{w}{2h_{cg}}\right)$ — result in degrees
- [x] Stopping distance: $d = v \cdot t_r + \frac{v^2}{2\mu g}$ — for loose/rough surfaces use $\mu = 0.3$ to $0.5$
- [x] Noise exposure: $T_{safe} = \frac{8}{2^{(L-85)/3}}$ hours per day (L in dBA)

### ✅ Golden Phrases for Full-Mark Answers

- "**Engineering controls are preferred over administrative controls, which are preferred over PPE.**"
- "**The operator is the last line of defense; the system should be designed so that operator error does not lead to fatality.**"
- "**Safety is not a priority but a value — priorities can change; values do not.**"
- "**No task is so urgent that it cannot be done safely.**" (Stop Work Authority principle)

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Exam Strategy:** For any "discuss safety" question, structure your answer as: **(1) Define, (2) Identify hazards, (3) List controls in Hierarchy order, (4) Cite standards, (5) Conclude with human/behavioral factor.** This template guarantees 80%+ marks on any safety question in KTU 2024 ESE.
